pattern: Subject + verb + object + 了
explanation_en: Use 了 at the end of a sentence to mark a completed action. In Taiwan Mandarin, 了 frequently appears at the end to indicate that something has been done. This is the most basic completion marker.
title: Completed Action with 了
meaning: Completed-action 了 (perfective aspect marker)
when_to_use: When talking about something you did (past completed action)
When confirming that an action has been done
When reporting daily activities
when_not_to_use: Do not use 了 for habitual or recurring actions
Do not use 了 with stative verbs like 是 (shì) or 在 (zài)
Do not use 了 when the sentence has a modal verb like 要 (yào) or 想 (xiǎng) in the main clause
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 了 is used frequently in casual conversation to mark completion. The position at the end of the sentence is the most natural for short declarative statements about daily activities.
word_order_notes: The basic structure is: Subject + Time + Verb + Object + 了. Time expressions like 昨天 (zuótiān) go before the verb.
english_contrast: English uses past tense ('I bought'). Chinese uses 了 after the object to mark completion. There is no tense conjugation in Chinese.
korean_contrast: Korean uses '~었/았다' as a past tense suffix on the verb. Chinese uses the particle 了 after the object instead.

pattern: 昨天 + subject + verb + object + 了
explanation_en: In Mandarin, time expressions like 昨天 (zuótiān, yesterday) always come BEFORE the verb, often at the start of the sentence or right after the subject. This is different from English where 'yesterday' can go at the end.
title: Time-First Word Order
meaning: Time expressions precede the verb in Mandarin.
when_to_use: When stating when something happened
When the time is the topic or context of the sentence
In both formal and casual speech in Taiwan
when_not_to_use: Do not place the time expression at the end of the sentence
Do not separate the time expression from the action it modifies
pragmatic_notes: Taiwan Mandarin consistently places time expressions before the verb. This is one of the most important word-order rules to master.
word_order_notes: Time expressions (昨天, 今天, 明天, 上個禮拜, etc.) always come BEFORE the verb phrase. The pattern is: Time + Subject + Verb + Object.
english_contrast: English allows 'I went out yesterday' (time at end). Chinese MUST have time before the verb: 我昨天出門了 (literally: I yesterday went out).
korean_contrast: Korean also places time before the verb (어제 나갔어요), which is similar to Chinese. However, Korean uses a past tense suffix while Chinese uses 了.

pattern: Verb + object + 了 (with time expression)
explanation_en: When a sentence has both a time expression and a specific object, the 了 goes after the object to mark completion of the whole verb phrase. This is the standard Taiwan Mandarin pattern for reporting completed daily activities.
title: Verb-Object-了 Pattern
meaning: 了 after object marks completion of the action on that object.
when_to_use: When reporting what you did with a specific object
When the object is definite or specific
In everyday conversation about completed tasks
when_not_to_use: Do not place 了 immediately after the verb if there is a specific object
Do not use 了 after 沒 (méi) in negative sentences about the past
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan casual speech, 了 at the end of a short declarative sentence is extremely common. It makes the sentence sound complete and natural.
word_order_notes: Time + Subject + Verb + Object + 了. All elements before 了 contribute to the completed action.
english_contrast: English: 'I ate dinner' (past tense on verb). Chinese: 我吃飯了 (I eat rice LE = I ate).
korean_contrast: Korean: '밥 먹었어요' (rice ate-past). Chinese: 我吃飯了 (I eat rice LE). Note the different position of the completion marker.

pattern: Clause 1，然後 + clause 2
explanation_en: Use 然後 (ránhòu) to connect two sequential actions, meaning 'and then' or 'after that'. It is one of the most frequently used connectors in Taiwan Mandarin conversation.
title: Sequencing with 然後
meaning: and then; after that
when_to_use: When narrating a sequence of events
When telling someone what you did step by step
In both casual and semi-formal conversation in Taiwan
when_not_to_use: Do not overuse 然後 as a filler word in every sentence
Do not place 然後 at the very beginning of the first action
pragmatic_notes: Taiwan speakers frequently use 然後 in everyday narration. It is natural and expected in casual storytelling.
word_order_notes: The first clause describes the first action, then 然後 introduces the second action. Both clauses should be complete verb phrases.
english_contrast: English uses 'and then' or 'and' between clauses. Chinese uses 然後 which is more explicit about sequence.
korean_contrast: Korean uses '그리고' (and) or '그 다음에' (after that). Chinese 然後 is closer to '그 다음에' in meaning.

pattern: 先 + action 1，然後 + action 2
explanation_en: The pair 先 (xiān)...然後 (ránhòu)... emphasizes the order of two sequential actions. 先 marks the first action as happening first, and 然後 introduces the second. This is a very common pattern for giving instructions or narrating sequences.
title: First...Then... with 先...然後...
meaning: first... and then...
when_to_use: When giving step-by-step instructions
When emphasizing that one action must happen before another
When narrating a planned sequence of events
when_not_to_use: Do not reverse the order — 先 always marks the first action
Do not use 先 without a second action (it implies there is more to come)
pragmatic_notes: Taiwan Mandarin uses 先...然後... frequently in daily conversation. It is a clear way to organize information.
word_order_notes: 先 + Verb Phrase 1 + 然後 + Verb Phrase 2. Both verb phrases should be complete.
english_contrast: English: 'First I eat, then I go.' Chinese: 我先吃飯，然後去。The 先 comes before the verb, unlike English 'first' which starts the clause.
korean_contrast: Korean: '먼저 밥을 먹고, 그 다음에 가요.' Chinese: 我先吃飯，然後去。Similar structure with first-action marker before the verb.

pattern: Review: 了 position in sequence sentences
explanation_en: Review the correct placement of 了 when combining sequence markers with completed actions. When narrating a sequence of past events, 了 typically appears at the end of each completed clause.
title: 了 Position Review in Sequences
meaning: Review: placing 了 correctly in multi-clause sentences.
when_to_use: When reviewing the 了 pattern in longer narratives
When combining 了 with 然後 in the same sentence
when_not_to_use: Do not place 了 before the object in sequence sentences
Do not omit 了 when the first action is clearly completed
pragmatic_notes: In narrative sequences, each completed action can take its own 了. The 了 goes after the object of each clause.
word_order_notes: Clause 1 + Object + 了，然後 + Clause 2 + Object + 了.
english_contrast: English uses past tense on each verb. Chinese places 了 after each object.
korean_contrast: Korean uses ~고 (connective) between past tense verbs. Chinese uses 了 + 然後.

pattern: Subject + verb + 過 + object
explanation_en: Use 過 (guò) after a verb to indicate that someone has had the experience of doing something. It means 'have ever done' and is used for life experiences, not for specific completed actions.
title: Experience Marker 過
meaning: have ever done (experiential aspect marker)
when_to_use: When asking if someone has ever done something
When sharing life experiences
When talking about things you have or haven't tried
when_not_to_use: Do not use 過 for a specific, one-time completed action (use 了 instead)
Do not use 過 for actions that are happening right now
過 is different from 了 — 了 marks completion, 過 marks experience
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, asking '你V過O嗎?' (Have you ever V-ed O?) is a common conversation starter and a natural way to get to know someone's experiences.
word_order_notes: Subject + Verb + 過 + Object. 過 comes directly after the verb and before the object.
english_contrast: English: 'Have you ever been to Taipei?' Chinese: 你去過臺北嗎？(You go-GUO Taipei MA?). The 過 is like 'have ever' in English.
korean_contrast: Korean: '~아/어 본 적이 있다' (have the experience of). Chinese 過 is similar to '~아/어 보다' in Korean.

pattern: Subject + 從來 + 沒有 + verb + 過 (+ object)
explanation_en: Use 從來沒有...過 (cónglái méiyǒu...guò) to say 'have never done something'. 從來 (cónglái) emphasizes 'ever/never' and strengthens the negation. This is a very common and natural pattern in Taiwan Mandarin.
title: Never Have with 從來沒有...過
meaning: have never done (strong negation of experience)
when_to_use: When you want to strongly say you have never done something
When expressing surprise that someone hasn't experienced something common
When contrasting your experience with someone else's
when_not_to_use: Do not use 了 with this pattern — 過 is already marking experience
Do not use 沒有...過 for things happening right now
pragmatic_notes: Taiwan speakers use 從來沒有...過 frequently. Adding 從來 makes the negation stronger and more natural in conversation.
word_order_notes: Subject + 從來 + 沒有 + Verb + 過 (+ Object). 從來 and 沒有 go before the verb, 過 goes after the verb.
english_contrast: English: 'I have never eaten this.' Chinese: 我從來沒有吃過這個。(I ever not-have eat-GUO this).
korean_contrast: Korean: '한 번도 ~아/어 본 적이 없다.' Chinese 從來沒有...過 is structurally similar to Korean's '한 번도...ㄴ 적이 없다.'

pattern: 以前 + subject + verb + 過 / Subject + 以前 + verb + 過
explanation_en: Use 以前 (yǐqián) to mean 'before' or 'previously'. When combined with 過, it emphasizes that the experience happened in the past. 以前 can go at the beginning of the sentence or after the subject.
title: Before/Previously with 以前
meaning: before; previously; ago
when_to_use: When contrasting past experience with present situation
When telling someone about your background
When the time frame of the experience matters
when_not_to_use: Do not confuse 以前 (before/ago) with 已經 (already)
Do not place 以前 after the verb
pragmatic_notes: 以前 is versatile. It can mean 'ago' (三天以前 = three days ago) or 'before/previously' depending on context.
word_order_notes: 以前 can be placed at the start of the sentence or after the subject. Both are correct: 以前我去過 / 我以前去過.
english_contrast: English: 'I have been there before.' Chinese: 我以前去過那裡。(I before go-GUO there).
korean_contrast: Korean: '전에 가 본 적이 있어요.' Chinese 以前 corresponds to Korean '전에'.

pattern: Subject + 在 + verb (+ object)
explanation_en: Use 在 (zài) before a verb to indicate an action in progress, similar to English '-ing'. This is the most common way to describe what someone is doing right now in Taiwan Mandarin.
title: Progressive Action with 在
meaning: in the middle of doing (progressive aspect marker)
when_to_use: When describing what someone is doing right now
When answering 'What are you doing?'
When giving a reason for being unavailable
when_not_to_use: Do not use 在 with stative verbs like 是 (shì), 知道 (zhīdào), 喜歡 (xǐhuān)
Do not confuse 在 (progressive) with 在 (location — 'at')
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan casual speech, 在 + verb is the most natural way to say 'I'm VERB-ing'. It's shorter and more common than 正在.
word_order_notes: Subject + 在 + Verb (+ Object). 在 goes directly before the action verb.
english_contrast: English: 'I am eating.' Chinese: 我在吃飯。(I ZAI eat rice). 在 functions like 'am -ing'.
korean_contrast: Korean: '~고 있다' (am doing). Chinese 在 is similar to Korean '~고 있다'.

pattern: Subject + 正在 + verb (+ object) (+ 呢)
explanation_en: 正在 (zhèngzài) is a more emphatic form of the progressive aspect, meaning 'right in the middle of doing'. Adding 呢 (ne) at the end softens the tone and makes it more conversational in Taiwan Mandarin.
title: Emphatic Progressive with 正在
meaning: right in the middle of doing (emphatic progressive)
when_to_use: When emphasizing that an action is happening at this exact moment
When you are busy and can't do something else
In slightly more formal narration
when_not_to_use: Do not use 正在 for habitual actions
正在 is more emphatic than 在 — use 在 for casual statements
pragmatic_notes: Taiwan speakers often add 呢 at the end of 正在 sentences to make them sound friendlier: 我正在吃飯呢 (I'm eating right now — friendly tone).
word_order_notes: Subject + 正在 + Verb (+ Object) + 呢. 呢 is optional but adds a conversational tone.
english_contrast: English: 'I am eating right now.' Chinese: 我正在吃飯呢。The 正在 emphasizes the 'right now' aspect.
korean_contrast: Korean: '지금 ~고 있어요.' Chinese 正在 is similar to Korean '지금' + '~고 있다'.

correct_examples.translation_en: I am reading a book right now.

negative_examples.error: This word order is not correct Mandarin.

transformation_drills: 把 '我在吃飯' 改成 '正在' 強調
加上 '呢' 讓語氣更自然
production_drills: 用 '正在...呢' 說你正在做什麼
解釋為什麼現在不能出門
common_error_patterns: Using zai with stative verbs like need, like, or know.
Forgetting that zai marks an action happening right now.
Putting zai after the verb.

repair_feedback.word_order: Put zai or zhengzai before the action verb.
repair_feedback.meaning: Use progressive markers only for ongoing actions, not states or preferences.
repair_feedback.practice: Sort five verbs into action-now or state, then make progressive sentences only for actions.

drill_examples.translation_en: I am reading a book right now.

pattern: Subject + 現在 + 在 + verb ／ 現在 + 很 + adjective
explanation_en: Use 現在 (xiànzài, 'now') with 在 to describe what is happening at the present moment, or with an adjective to describe the current state. This is a very practical pattern for daily communication.
title: Now / Currently with 現在
meaning: now; at the moment
when_to_use: When stating what is happening now
When describing current status or mood
When explaining why you can or can't do something
when_not_to_use: Do not confuse 現在 (now) with 正在 (right in the middle of)
現在 can be used with adjectives: 現在很忙 (now very busy)
pragmatic_notes: 現在 is one of the most useful time words. Combined with 在 + verb, it clearly anchors the action to the present moment.
word_order_notes: 現在 + Subject + 在 + Verb / Subject + 現在 + 很 + Adjective.
english_contrast: English: 'I am busy now.' Chinese: 我現在很忙。(I now very busy). Notice no 'am' is needed with adjectives.
korean_contrast: Korean: '지금 바빠요.' (now busy). Chinese 現在 corresponds directly to Korean '지금'.

correct_examples.translation_en: I am watching TV now.
He is very busy now.

negative_examples.error: This word order is not correct Mandarin.

Putting zai after the verb.

repair_feedback.meaning: Use progressive markers only for ongoing actions, not states or preferences.
repair_feedback.practice: Sort five verbs into action-now or state, then make progressive sentences only for actions.

pattern: Subject + 會 + verb (+ object)
explanation_en: Use 會 (huì) to express learned ability or skill — things you know how to do because you learned them. This is different from 可以 (kěyǐ) which is about permission, and 能 (néng) which is about physical capability.
title: Learned Ability with 會
meaning: can; know how to (learned skill)
when_to_use: When talking about skills you have learned
When asking if someone knows how to do something
When describing abilities like speaking a language, cooking, driving
when_not_to_use: Do not use 會 for physical capability (use 能 néng)
Do not use 會 for permission (use 可以 kěyǐ)
會 also means 'will' for future — context distinguishes the meaning
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 你會說中文嗎？(Can you speak Chinese?) is one of the most common questions asked of foreigners. 會 is the standard word for language ability.
word_order_notes: Subject + 會 + Verb (+ Object). 會 goes directly before the action verb.
english_contrast: English: 'I can speak Chinese.' Chinese: 我會說中文。(I HUI speak Chinese). 會 is like 'can' for learned skills.
korean_contrast: Korean: '~할 줄 알다' (know how to do). Chinese 會 corresponds to Korean '~ㄹ 줄 알다'.

correct_examples.pinyin: Wǒ huì shuō yī diǎn Zhōngwén.
correct_examples.translation_en: I can speak a little Chinese.
He can cook Taiwanese food.
Can you drive?

negative_examples.error: This word order is not correct Mandarin.

repair_feedback.word_order: Use ruguo + condition, jiu + result when the result follows naturally.
repair_feedback.meaning: The first clause sets the condition; the second clause says what will happen.
repair_feedback.practice: Repair two English if sentences into ruguo...jiu... Chinese order.

drill_examples.pinyin: Wǒ huì shuō yī diǎn Zhōngwén.
drill_examples.translation_en: I can speak a little Chinese.

pattern: Subject + 不會 + verb (+ object)
explanation_en: Use 不會 (bú huì) to say you cannot do something (a learned skill) or don't know how to do it. 不 negates 會 directly. This is the standard way to express lack of ability.
title: Cannot / Don't Know How with 不會
meaning: cannot; don't know how to (negation of learned ability)
when_to_use: When you want to say you don't know how to do something
When admitting a skill you haven't learned yet
When politely declining because you lack the skill
when_not_to_use: Do not use 不會 for things you're not allowed to do (use 不可以)
Do not use 不會 for physical inability (use 不能)
pragmatic_notes: Taiwan speakers often soften 我不會 with 不太會 (not very good at) to be more modest: 我不太會說中文 (I can't really speak Chinese well).
word_order_notes: Subject + 不 + 會 + Verb (+ Object). 不 negates 會 directly, then the verb follows.
english_contrast: English: 'I can't swim.' Chinese: 我不會游泳。(I not-HUI swim).
korean_contrast: Korean: '~할 줄 모르다' (don't know how to do). Chinese 不會 corresponds to Korean '~ㄹ 줄 모르다'.

pattern: Subject + 還不太會 + verb / Subject + 還不太 + adjective
explanation_en: Use 還不太 (hái bú tài) to soften a negative statement, meaning 'not quite yet' or 'still not very'. 還不太會 means 'still not very good at'. This is a very Taiwan-style way to be modest and polite.
title: Not Quite Yet with 還不太
meaning: still not very; not quite yet (softened negation)
when_to_use: When you want to modestly say you're not good at something
When something is still in progress but not there yet
When you can do something a little but not well
when_not_to_use: Do not use 還不太 for things you definitely cannot do (use 不會)
還不太 implies you are learning or it's changeable
pragmatic_notes: Taiwan culture values modesty. 還不太會 is a polite way to downplay your abilities even when you can actually do something reasonably well.
word_order_notes: Subject + 還 + 不太 + 會 + Verb / Subject + 還 + 不太 + Adjective.
english_contrast: English: 'I still can't speak very well.' Chinese: 我還不太會說。(I still not-too-HUI speak).
korean_contrast: Korean: '아직 잘 못해요.' Chinese 還不太 is similar to Korean '아직 잘 못~'.

correct_examples.pinyin: Wǒ hái bú tài huì shuō Zhōngwén.
correct_examples.translation_en: I still can't speak Chinese very well.
This is still not very hot.

negative_examples.error: This word order is not correct Mandarin.

pattern: Modifier + 的 + noun
explanation_en: Use 的 (de) to connect a modifier (adjective, noun, or clause) to a noun. This is the most versatile particle in Chinese. It can mark possession (我的書 = my book), description (好吃的東西 = delicious food), and relative clauses.
title: Modifier Particle 的
meaning: possessive/descriptive/modifier particle
when_to_use: When describing a noun with an adjective
When showing possession (my, your, his, etc.)
When creating a longer description before a noun
In practically every conversation in Taiwan
when_not_to_use: Do not use 的 with single-syllable adjectives + single-syllable nouns in very close relationships (好朋友, not 好的朋友)
Do not use 的 after 這 (zhè) or 那 (nà) when followed directly by a noun
pragmatic_notes: Taiwan Mandarin uses 的 extensively. Mastering 的 is essential for natural speech. It's the most frequently used character in Chinese.
word_order_notes: Modifier + 的 + Noun. The modifier can be an adjective, a noun (possessive), or a full clause.
english_contrast: English: 'delicious food' — adjective before noun. Chinese: 好吃的東西 (delicious DE thing). 的 is like a connector.
korean_contrast: Korean: '맛있는 음식' uses '~ㄴ/은' as modifier. Chinese 的 functions similarly to Korean's adnominal endings.

pattern: Subject + 很 + adjective
explanation_en: In Chinese, adjectives are predicates and need 很 (hěn) before them in declarative sentences. While 很 literally means 'very', in this structure it often functions as a grammatical linker rather than carrying strong 'very' meaning. Without 很, the sentence sounds incomplete or comparative.
title: Adjective Predicates with 很
meaning: very; grammatical linker for adjectives
when_to_use: When describing something with an adjective
In almost every descriptive sentence in Chinese
When 很 doesn't necessarily mean 'very' but just links subject to adjective
when_not_to_use: Do not add 是 before adjectives — Chinese adjectives don't need 'to be'
Do use 很 even when you don't mean 'very' — it's grammatically required in most cases
pragmatic_notes: Taiwan speakers use 很 so frequently with adjectives that it often loses its 'very' meaning and just becomes a grammatical connector. To really say 'very', you can use 非常 (fēicháng) or 很 with emphasis.
word_order_notes: Subject + 很 + Adjective. No verb 'to be' is needed.
english_contrast: English: 'This is delicious.' Chinese: 這個很好吃。(This HEN delicious). No 'is' — 很 connects subject to adjective.
korean_contrast: Korean: '이거 맛있어요.' (this delicious). Chinese: 這個很好吃。Both drop the copula with adjectives.

pattern: 那 + measure word + noun + 的 + description
explanation_en: Use 那個 (nà ge) / 那種 (nà zhǒng) to point out a specific person or thing and then describe it with 的. This pattern is essential for identifying and describing things in conversation.
title: That (Specific Reference) with 那個
meaning: that (specific thing/person) + description
when_to_use: When pointing out something specific
When identifying a person by their characteristics
When comparing or distinguishing between items
when_not_to_use: Do not drop the measure word — 那書 is incorrect, use 那本書
Different nouns take different measure words
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 那個 (nèige in casual speech) is extremely common both as a determiner ('that one') and as a filler word in conversation.
word_order_notes: 那 + Measure Word + Noun + 的 + Description.
english_contrast: English: 'That person wearing a hat.' Chinese: 那個戴帽子的人。(That GE wear hat DE person).
korean_contrast: Korean: '저 모자 쓴 사람.' Chinese 那 + MW + N + 的 corresponds to Korean '저/그 + N'.

pattern: A + 比 + B + adjective
explanation_en: Use 比 (bǐ) to compare two things, meaning 'A is more ADJ than B'. This is the fundamental comparison pattern in Chinese. It is extremely common in daily Taiwan conversation for comparing prices, quality, speed, etc.
title: Comparison with 比
meaning: compared to; more than (comparison marker)
when_to_use: When comparing two things (prices, quality, people, places)
When making shopping decisions
When discussing preferences and choices
when_not_to_use: Do not use 很 before the adjective in a 比 comparison
Do not use 比 for equality (use 跟...一樣 instead)
The adjective after 比 must be a gradable adjective
pragmatic_notes: Taiwan speakers use 比 constantly when shopping, discussing food, and comparing options. It's one of the most practical patterns for daily life.
word_order_notes: A + 比 + B + Adjective. The adjective comes AFTER both nouns, unlike English 'A is ADJ-er than B'.
english_contrast: English: 'A is cheaper than B.' Chinese: A比B便宜 (A compared-to B cheap). No 'more' — the 比 structure already implies comparison.
korean_contrast: Korean: 'A가 B보다 싸다.' Chinese 比 is similar to Korean '보다'.

pattern: Subject + 比較 + adjective / A + 比 + B + 比較 + adjective
explanation_en: Use 比較 (bǐjiào) to mean 'comparatively' or 'relatively'. It can be used alone to say something is 'more ADJ' in a general sense, or combined with 比 to soften the comparison. In Taiwan, 比較 is extremely common in everyday speech.
title: Comparatively with 比較
meaning: comparatively; relatively; more
when_to_use: When saying something is comparatively more [adjective]
When softening a comparison to be polite
When making a general comparison without explicitly naming B
when_not_to_use: Do not use 比較 with extreme adjectives
比較 can be used without 比 for a general comparison
pragmatic_notes: Taiwan speakers love 比較. It's a softer, more flexible way to compare than the strict 比 pattern. '這個比較好' (This one is better) is heard all the time.
word_order_notes: 比較 + Adjective / 比 + B + 比較 + Adjective.
english_contrast: English: 'This one is better.' Chinese: 這個比較好。(This compare good). 比較 adds the comparative meaning.
korean_contrast: Korean: '이게 더 좋아요.' Chinese 比較 corresponds to Korean '더' (more).

pattern: A + 沒有 + B + (那麼) + adjective
explanation_en: Use 沒有 (méiyǒu) for negative comparison: 'A is not as ADJ as B'. Adding 那麼 (nàme, 'that/so') before the adjective is common and natural. This pattern is used when A falls short of B in some quality.
title: Negative Comparison with 沒有
meaning: not as...as (negative comparison)
when_to_use: When saying something is not as good/big/expensive as something else
When managing expectations or making a modest comparison
When correcting an assumption about relative quality
when_not_to_use: Do not use 不比 for simple negative comparison — 不比 implies 'not more than' which has a different nuance
Do not drop 那麼 in most cases — it makes the sentence more natural
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 沒有...那麼... is the go-to pattern for 'not as X as Y'. It's straightforward and widely used.
word_order_notes: A + 沒有 + B + 那麼 + Adjective.

korean_contrast: Korean: '이게 저것만큼 비싸지 않아요.' Chinese 沒有...那麼 corresponds to Korean '~만큼 ~지 않다'.

pattern: Subject + 常常 + verb (+ object)
explanation_en: Use 常常 (chángcháng) before a verb to indicate that an action happens often or frequently. It is one of the most common frequency adverbs in Taiwan Mandarin.
title: Often/Frequently with 常常
meaning: often; frequently
when_to_use: When describing habits and routines
When talking about things that happen regularly
When contrasting frequency of different activities
when_not_to_use: Do not use 常常 for one-time events
常常 goes before the verb, never at the end
pragmatic_notes: Taiwan speakers frequently use 常常 in daily conversation about routines, habits, and preferences. 常 alone can also be used in more casual speech.
word_order_notes: Subject + 常常 + Verb (+ Object). Frequency adverbs always go before the verb.
english_contrast: English: 'I often go there.' Chinese: 我常常去那裡。(I often go there). Same word order.
korean_contrast: Korean: '자주 가요.' Chinese 常常 corresponds to Korean '자주'.

correct_examples.pinyin: Wǒ chángcháng qù nà jiā kāfēi diàn.

He often works overtime.

pattern: Subject + 有時候 + verb / 有時候 + subject + verb
explanation_en: Use 有時候 (yǒu shíhou) to mean 'sometimes'. It can go before or after the subject. This is one of the most natural-sounding frequency expressions in Taiwan Mandarin.
title: Sometimes with 有時候
meaning: sometimes; at times
when_to_use: When something happens occasionally but not regularly
When contrasting with 常常 (often) or 從來不 (never)
When describing variable situations
when_not_to_use: Do not confuse 有時候 (sometimes) with 有的時候 (there are times when)
有時候 is more casual — use 有時 in formal writing
pragmatic_notes: 有時候 is very commonly used in Taiwan conversation. It's more natural than the shorter 有時 in casual speech.
word_order_notes: 有時候 + Clause / Subject + 有時候 + Verb.
english_contrast: English: 'Sometimes I don't understand.' Chinese: 我有時候聽不懂。(I sometimes listen-not-understand).
korean_contrast: Korean: '가끔 이해 못해요.' Chinese 有時候 corresponds to Korean '가끔'.

pattern: Subject + 喜歡 + verb + object / Subject + 喜歡 + noun
explanation_en: Use 喜歡 (xǐhuān) to express liking something or enjoying doing something. 喜歡 can be followed by a noun (我喜歡咖啡) or a verb phrase (我喜歡喝咖啡). This is an essential pattern for expressing preferences.
title: Like/Enjoy with 喜歡
meaning: to like; to enjoy; to be fond of
when_to_use: When expressing preferences and tastes
When talking about hobbies and free time activities
When choosing between options
when_not_to_use: Do not use 很 with 喜歡 to mean 'like' — 很喜歡 means 'really like'
喜歡 + verb is the correct pattern for 'like doing'
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 喜歡 is used frequently. Adding 很 before it (很喜歡) is the natural way to say you like something, not just to say 'really like'.
word_order_notes: Subject + 喜歡 + Noun / Subject + 喜歡 + Verb + Object.
english_contrast: English: 'I like drinking coffee.' Chinese: 我喜歡喝咖啡。(I like drink coffee). Both languages use the same structure.
korean_contrast: Korean: '커피 마시는 것을 좋아해요.' Chinese 喜歡 corresponds to Korean '좋아하다'.

correct_examples.translation_en: I like listening to music.
She likes watching movies.

pattern: Subject + 要不要 + verb (+ object) + ？
explanation_en: Use 要不要 (yào bú yào) to make a suggestion or invitation, meaning 'Do you want to...?' or 'How about...?'. This is one of the most common ways to suggest an activity in Taiwan.
title: Suggestions with 要不要
meaning: Do you want to...? / How about...?
when_to_use: When suggesting an activity to someone
When inviting someone to do something together
When offering something to someone
when_not_to_use: Do not use 要不要 for formal invitations — use 要不要一起... for casual suggestions
Do not confuse with 想不想 (xiǎng bù xiǎng) which is about desire, not suggestion
pragmatic_notes: Taiwan speakers use 要不要 constantly for casual invitations. It's friendly and natural. Adding 一起 (yīqǐ, together) makes it extra warm: 要不要一起去？
word_order_notes: Subject + 要不要 + Verb (+ Object) + ?
english_contrast: English: 'Do you want to eat?' Chinese: 你要不要吃飯？(You want-not-want eat rice?). The A-not-A form replaces 'Do you...?'
korean_contrast: Korean: '~을래요?' Chinese 要不要 corresponds to Korean '~을래요?'.

pattern: Subject + 該 + verb + 了
explanation_en: Use 該 (gāi) to mean 'should' or 'it's time to'. Adding 了 at the end emphasizes that the time has come. 該...了 is the most natural way to say 'it's time to...' in Taiwan Mandarin.
title: It's Time To with 該...了
meaning: should; ought to; it's time to
when_to_use: When saying it's time to do something
When suggesting someone should do something
When talking about obligations and schedules
when_not_to_use: Do not confuse 該 (should/time to) with 應該 (should/ought to) — 該...了 specifically means 'it's time'
該 can sound direct — add 吧 to soften: 該走了吧 (it's probably time to go, right?)
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 該...了 is commonly used for daily transitions: 該吃飯了 (time to eat), 該回家了 (time to go home), 該睡覺了 (time to sleep).
word_order_notes: Subject + 該 + Verb (+ Object) + 了.
english_contrast: English: 'It's time to go.' Chinese: 該走了。(Should go LE). 該 + 了 means 'it's time to'.
korean_contrast: Korean: '~할 시간이야.' Chinese 該...了 corresponds to Korean '~할 시간이다'.

pattern: 不好意思 + request / 不好意思，+ statement
explanation_en: Use 不好意思 (bù hǎo yìsi) to politely get someone's attention, apologize for a minor inconvenience, or preface a request. It is the Swiss Army knife of Taiwan politeness — used constantly in daily life.
title: Excuse Me / Sorry with 不好意思
meaning: excuse me; sorry; pardon me (polite opener)
when_to_use: When getting someone's attention politely
When apologizing for a minor inconvenience
When making a request of a stranger
When declining something politely
when_not_to_use: Do not use 不好意思 for serious apologies — use 對不起 (duìbuqǐ) for genuine mistakes
Do not overuse in very casual settings with close friends
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 不好意思 is heard everywhere — in shops, on the MRT, in restaurants. It's the most versatile polite expression. You can start almost any request with it.
word_order_notes: 不好意思，+ Request/Sentence.
english_contrast: English: 'Excuse me, could you...?' Chinese: 不好意思，可以...嗎？
korean_contrast: Korean: '실례합니다 / 죄송합니다.' Chinese 不好意思 is between Korean '실례합니다' and '죄송합니다' in politeness.

pattern: Body part + 不舒服 / Body part + 痛 / Body part + 不舒服，+ description
explanation_en: Use 不舒服 (bù shūfu, 'uncomfortable') or 痛 (tòng, 'painful/hurts') after a body part to describe physical discomfort. This is essential for visiting a doctor or pharmacy in Taiwan.
title: Body Aches with 不舒服 / 痛
meaning: uncomfortable; painful; hurts
when_to_use: When describing physical symptoms
When visiting a doctor or pharmacy
When explaining why you can't do something
when_not_to_use: Do not use 不舒服 for emotional discomfort (use 心情不好)
痛 is for acute pain, 不舒服 is for general discomfort
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, knowing how to describe symptoms in Chinese is very practical. Pharmacies are common and pharmacists can give advice for minor issues.

korean_contrast: Korean: '머리가 아파요.' Chinese 痛/不舒服 corresponds to Korean '아프다/불편하다'.

meaning: a little; slightly; somewhat
when_to_use: When describing mild symptoms or discomfort
When softening a negative description
When something is slightly different from expected
when_not_to_use: Do not use 有點 with positive adjectives (not 有點好吃 — use 還不錯 instead)
有點 is typically used with negative or neutral descriptions

pattern: 幾 + measure word + noun / 幾天 / 幾個
explanation_en: Use 幾 (jǐ) to ask 'how many' or 'a few'. 幾天 (jǐ tiān) can mean 'how many days' or 'a few days' depending on context. In statements, it typically means 'a few', and in questions, 'how many'.
title: How Many / A Few with 幾
meaning: how many; a few; several
when_to_use: When asking about quantity (how many days/hours/people)
When describing a small, unspecified number (a few days)
In medical contexts: 'How many days have you had this?'
when_not_to_use: Do not confuse 幾 (how many, usually <10) with 多少 (duōshao, how many, any amount)
幾 requires a measure word: 幾個人, not 幾人
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 幾天 is frequently used both as a question and as a statement. Context and intonation distinguish the two uses.
word_order_notes: 幾 + Measure Word + Noun.
english_contrast: English: 'a few days' / 'how many days?' Chinese: 幾天 — same phrase, different context.
korean_contrast: Korean: '며칠' (a few days / how many days). Chinese 幾天 is similar to Korean '며칠'.

pattern: Subject + 要 + verb (+ object) / Subject + 要 + noun
explanation_en: Use 要 (yào) to express 'need' or 'want'. In medical contexts, 要 often means 'need to' (take medicine, see a doctor). 要 can also mean 'will' depending on context.
title: Need / Want with 要
meaning: to need; to want; will; going to
when_to_use: When you need to do something (take medicine, rest)
When requesting something at a pharmacy or clinic
When expressing intention or necessity
when_not_to_use: Do not confuse 要 (need/want) with 想 (would like — softer)
要 is more direct than 想 — use 想 for polite requests
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 我要買藥 (I need to buy medicine) is direct and common at pharmacies. 我要 + verb is a very frequent pattern in daily life.
word_order_notes: Subject + 要 + Verb (+ Object) / Subject + 要 + Noun.
english_contrast: English: 'I need to buy medicine.' Chinese: 我要買藥。(I want/need buy medicine). 要 can mean both want and need.
korean_contrast: Korean: '약을 사야 해요.' Chinese 要 corresponds to both Korean '~해야 하다' (must) and '~고 싶다' (want).

pattern: 每天 + verb + number + 次 / 一天 + number + 次
explanation_en: Use 次 (cì) as a measure word for occurrences or times. 一天三次 (yī tiān sān cì) means 'three times a day'. This is essential for understanding medication instructions in Taiwan.

meaning: times; occurrences (frequency measure word)
when_to_use: When talking about medication frequency
When describing how often you do something
When giving or receiving instructions about frequency
when_not_to_use: Do not confuse 次 (times/occurrences) with 遍 (biàn, times through from start to finish)
次 is for counting occurrences, 遍 is for counting complete repetitions
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan pharmacies, medication instructions often say 一天三次 (three times a day) or 每天兩次 (twice a day). Understanding 次 is practical for daily life.

When asking a pharmacist or doctor for guidance
When confirming instructions

pattern: Place + 有 + facility / Place + 沒有 + facility
explanation_en: Use 有 (yǒu) to indicate that a place has something (a facility, feature, or amenity). 沒有 (méiyǒu) means the place doesn't have it. This pattern is essential for describing locations in Taiwan.
title: Place Has / Doesn't Have with 有/沒有
meaning: has; there is / doesn't have; there isn't
when_to_use: When describing what facilities a place has
When asking about amenities (WiFi, bathroom, etc.)
When comparing different locations
when_not_to_use: Do not use 是 for existence — 是 is for identity, 有 is for possession/existence
Place + 有 + thing is the standard pattern
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, asking '這裡有WiFi嗎？' (Does this place have WiFi?) or '有廁所嗎？' (Is there a bathroom?) are daily-life essentials.

pattern: 在 + location + 有 + thing / Location + 有 + thing
explanation_en: Use 在...有 (zài...yǒu) to say 'at [location] there is [thing]'. This pattern combines location marking with existence. 在 marks the location, 有 marks existence.
title: At Location There Is with 在...有
meaning: at [location] there is...
when_to_use: When describing what exists at a specific location
When giving directions that reference landmarks
When explaining where something can be found
when_not_to_use: Do not use 在 and 有 in the wrong order — 在 comes first for location

pattern: Thing + 壞了 / Thing + 不能用 / Thing + 有問題
explanation_en: Use 壞了 (huài le) to say something is broken or not working. 不能用 (bù néng yòng) means 'can't use'. 有問題 (yǒu wèntí) means 'has a problem'. These are essential for reporting issues in Taiwan.
title: Broken / Not Working
meaning: broken; out of order; not working
when_to_use: When something is broken or not functioning
When reporting an issue to staff or a landlord
When explaining why you can't use something
when_not_to_use: Do not use 破了 (pò le, torn/broken through) for machines — use 壞了
壞了 is for malfunction, 破了 is for physical breaking/tearing
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, if something in your apartment or at a store isn't working, 壞了 is the word to use. It's direct and clear.
word_order_notes: Thing + 壞了 / Thing + 不能用.
english_contrast: English: 'The air conditioner is broken.' Chinese: 冷氣壞了。(AC broken LE).

pattern: 請 + verb / 請你 + verb / 麻煩你 + verb
explanation_en: Use 請 (qǐng) or 麻煩你 (máfan nǐ) to politely ask someone to do something. 請 is 'please', 麻煩你 is 'may I trouble you to...' — slightly more polite. Both are essential for daily interactions in Taiwan.
title: Polite Requests with 請 / 麻煩你
meaning: please; may I trouble you to...
when_to_use: When asking someone for help
When making a polite request of a stranger
In service situations (restaurants, stores, etc.)
when_not_to_use: Do not use 請 alone without a verb — it needs an action
麻煩你 is more deferential than 請 alone
pragmatic_notes: Taiwan service culture is very polite. Starting a request with 麻煩你 or 請 is expected and appreciated. It's one of the most useful phrases to know.
word_order_notes: 請 (+ 你) + Verb (+ Object) / 麻煩你 + Verb (+ Object).

explanation_en: Use 什麼時候 (shénme shíhou) to ask 'when' or 'what time'. Combined with 可以, it asks 'when can I...?' This is essential for scheduling and asking about availability in Taiwan.

pattern: Subject + 發生 + 了 + event / Event + 發生了
explanation_en: Use 發生 (fāshēng) to mean 'happen' or 'occur'. It is used for events, incidents, and things that took place. 發生了 marks a completed occurrence.

meaning: to happen; to occur; to take place

pattern: 結果 + result / Cause，結果 + result
explanation_en: Use 結果 (jiéguǒ) to mean 'as a result' or 'in the end'. It connects a situation or cause to its outcome. This is essential for storytelling and explaining sequences of events.

meaning: as a result; in the end; it turned out that
when_to_use: When explaining what happened as a result of something
When telling a story with an unexpected outcome
When describing the conclusion of a sequence
when_not_to_use: Do not confuse 結果 (result) with 所以 (therefore) — 結果 is more about the actual outcome
結果 often implies the outcome was different from what was expected

pattern: 因為 + reason，所以 + result
explanation_en: Use 因為...所以... (yīnwèi...suǒyǐ...) to express 'because...therefore...'. This is the standard cause-and-effect pattern. In Taiwan casual speech, either 因為 or 所以 can be used alone, but together they form a complete logical structure.
title: Because...Therefore with 因為...所以...
meaning: because... therefore...
when_to_use: When explaining reasons and results
When making a logical argument
When answering 'why' questions
when_not_to_use: Do not use 所以 at the beginning of the first clause — it always introduces the result
In casual speech, 因為 alone or 所以 alone is fine
pragmatic_notes: Taiwan speakers often drop 所以 in casual conversation, using just 因為. But in more structured explanations, the full pair sounds more complete.
word_order_notes: 因為 + Reason，所以 + Result.

korean_contrast: Korean: '비가 와서 안 갔어요.' Chinese 因為...所以 corresponds to Korean '~서/~니까'.

negative_examples.error: This word order is not correct Mandarin.

transformation_drills: 用 '因為...所以...' 解釋原因
只用 '所以' 不用 '因為'
common_error_patterns: Using yinwei without a clear result clause.
Putting suoyi before the reason instead of before the result.
Translating English because too literally and making a fragment.

repair_feedback.word_order: Use yinwei + reason, suoyi + result. In short answers, the reason alone can be enough.
repair_feedback.meaning: Make sure the listener can hear both cause and result.
repair_feedback.practice: Answer why questions with one short reason and one full yinwei...suoyi... sentence.

drill_examples.text: 因為下雨了，所以我沒去。

pattern: 幫 + person + verb / 幫我 + verb
explanation_en: Use 幫 (bāng) to mean 'help' or 'for (someone)'. 幫我 + verb means 'help me do X' or 'do X for me'. This is one of the most practical verbs in daily Taiwan life.
title: Help / Do For with 幫
meaning: to help; to do for (someone)
when_to_use: When asking someone to help you do something
When offering to do something for someone
In service interactions (stores, restaurants)
when_not_to_use: Do not confuse 幫 (help/for) with 給 (give)
幫 can mean 'on behalf of' as well as 'help'
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 幫我... is heard all day long: 幫我拿一下 (grab that for me), 幫我點餐 (order for me), 幫我看看 (take a look for me). It's essential for daily life.
word_order_notes: 幫 + Person + Verb (+ Object).
english_contrast: English: 'Help me carry this.' Chinese: 幫我拿這個。(Help me carry this).
korean_contrast: Korean: '이것 좀 들어주세요.' Chinese 幫 corresponds to Korean '~아/어 주다'.

correct_examples.pinyin: Bāng wǒ ná yīxià nà ge.
correct_examples.translation_en: Help me grab that for a moment.
I'll order for you.

negative_examples.error: This word order is not correct Mandarin.

用 '幫你' 主動提供幫助
common_error_patterns: Forgetting jiu in the result clause after ruguo.
Putting ruguo in both clauses.
Using English if word order and losing the result marker.

repair_feedback.word_order: Use ruguo + condition, jiu + result when the result follows naturally.
repair_feedback.meaning: The first clause sets the condition; the second clause says what will happen.
repair_feedback.practice: Repair two English if sentences into ruguo...jiu... Chinese order.

pattern: Subject + 需要 + noun/verb
explanation_en: Use 需要 (xūyào) to express 'need' or 'require'. It is more formal than 要 and is used for genuine necessities. 需要 can be followed by a noun or a verb phrase.
title: Need/Require with 需要
meaning: to need; to require
when_to_use: When expressing genuine needs
When making formal requests
When describing requirements
when_not_to_use: Do not use 需要 for casual wants — use 想要 (xiǎng yào) instead
需要 is stronger and more formal than 要
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 我需要幫助 (I need help) is a clear and direct phrase. 需要 is more appropriate for serious needs than the casual 要.
word_order_notes: Subject + 需要 + Noun / Verb Phrase.
english_contrast: English: 'I need help.' Chinese: 我需要幫忙。(I need help).
korean_contrast: Korean: '도움이 필요해요.' Chinese 需要 corresponds to Korean '필요하다'.

correct_examples.translation_en: I need help.
Do you need anything?

negative_examples.error: This word order is not correct Mandarin.

transformation_drills: 用 '需要' 說你需要什麼
用 '需要幫忙嗎？' 主動問
common_error_patterns: Choosing lai/qu from English perspective instead of speaker location.
Forgetting the direction complement after the main verb.
Using bring/take English one-to-one without checking movement direction.

repair_feedback.word_order: Verb + direction complement shows movement toward or away from the speaker/reference point.
repair_feedback.meaning: First choose the reference point; then choose lai or qu.
repair_feedback.practice: Describe moving one object toward you and one object away from you.

drill_examples.translation_en: I need help.
Do you need anything?

pattern: 可以 + 幫我 + verb + 嗎？/ 可不可以 + 幫我 + verb？
explanation_en: Use 可以幫我...嗎 (kěyǐ bāng wǒ...ma) to politely ask 'Can you help me...?' This combines the permission/possibility 可以 with the helpful-action 幫. It's the most common way to ask for assistance in Taiwan.
title: Can You Help Me with 可以幫我...嗎
meaning: Can you help me...?
when_to_use: When politely asking for assistance
When you need someone to do something for you
In almost any service or help situation in Taiwan
when_not_to_use: Do not use 要幫我 (want to help me) — use 可以幫我 for polite requests
Adding 不好意思 or 麻煩你 before this pattern makes it extra polite
pragmatic_notes: Taiwan speakers often say '不好意思，可以幫我...嗎？' as a complete polite request. It's the gold standard for asking for help from strangers.
word_order_notes: 可以 + 幫我 + Verb (+ Object) + 嗎？
english_contrast: English: 'Can you help me carry this?' Chinese: 可以幫我拿這個嗎？(Can help me carry this MA?).
korean_contrast: Korean: '이것 좀 들어주실 수 있나요?' Chinese 可以幫我...嗎 is similar to Korean '~아/어 주실 수 있나요?'.

pattern: Subject + 想 + verb (+ object)
explanation_en: Use 想 (xiǎng) to express 'want to' or 'would like to'. It is softer and more polite than 要 (yào). 想 is the preferred word for expressing desires and preferences in Taiwan.
title: Want/Would Like with 想
meaning: to want to; would like to; to think
when_to_use: When expressing a desire or preference
When making a polite request
When talking about future plans (softer than 要)
when_not_to_use: Do not use 想 for strong necessity — use 要 or 需要
想 can also mean 'to think' or 'to miss (someone)' — context distinguishes
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 我想... is the standard way to start expressing what you want. It's softer and more polite than 我要... which can sound demanding.
word_order_notes: Subject + 想 + Verb (+ Object).
english_contrast: English: 'I want to go home.' Chinese: 我想回家。(I want return home). 想 is 'want/would like'.
korean_contrast: Korean: '집에 가고 싶어요.' Chinese 想 corresponds to Korean '~고 싶다'.

correct_examples.translation_en: I want to go home.
What do you want to eat?
He wants to change jobs.

negative_examples.error: This word order is not correct Mandarin.

Using the new pattern where a simpler older pattern would be more natural.
Forgetting the function word that makes the pattern work.

repair_feedback.word_order: Say the core Chinese frame first, then fill in subject, time, verb, and object.
repair_feedback.meaning: Choose the pattern by meaning: time, reason, contrast, comparison, condition, or result.
repair_feedback.practice: Make one controlled sentence, one question, and one short answer with this pattern.

pattern: Subject + 有問題 / 跟 + person/thing + 有問題 / 關於 + topic + 有問題
explanation_en: Use 有問題 (yǒu wèntí) to say 'has a problem' or 'there is a problem'. This is the most direct way to flag an issue in Taiwan. It can be used for machines, situations, or interpersonal issues.
title: Has a Problem with 有問題
meaning: has a problem; there is an issue
when_to_use: When something isn't working right
When you have a concern or issue to raise
When asking if everything is okay
when_not_to_use: Do not use 有問題 for small preferences — it implies a real problem
有問題 can sound serious — soften with 有點問題 for minor issues
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 有問題嗎？(Is there a problem?) can sound confrontational. 有什麼問題嗎？(What's the problem?) is softer. For reporting issues, 這個有問題 (This has a problem) is direct and clear.
word_order_notes: Subject + 有問題 / 跟 + Person + 有問題.
english_contrast: English: 'There is a problem with this.' Chinese: 這個有問題。(This has problem).
korean_contrast: Korean: '이거 문제가 있어요.' Chinese 有問題 corresponds to Korean '문제가 있다'.

pattern: 可以 + 換 + object + 嗎？/ 可不可以 + 換？
explanation_en: Use 換 (huàn) to mean 'change', 'exchange', or 'swap'. 可以換...嗎 is how you ask to exchange something (a defective product, a different size, etc.) in Taiwan.
title: Can I Change/Exchange with 換
meaning: to change; to exchange; to swap
when_to_use: When you want to exchange a product
When you want to change something (plans, seats, etc.)
When asking for a different option
when_not_to_use: Do not confuse 換 (exchange) with 改 (change/alter)
換 is for swapping one thing for another, 改 is for modifying something
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan stores, 可以換嗎？(Can I exchange this?) is a common and acceptable request. Most stores have exchange policies.
word_order_notes: 可以 + 換 + Object + 嗎？
english_contrast: English: 'Can I exchange this?' Chinese: 可以換這個嗎？(Can exchange this MA?).
korean_contrast: Korean: '이거 교환할 수 있나요?' Chinese 換 corresponds to Korean '교환하다/바꾸다'.

repair_feedback.meaning: The first clause sets the condition; the second clause says what will happen.

pattern: Subject + 預約 + 了 + time/place / 跟 + place + 預約 + 了
explanation_en: Use 預約 (yùyuē) to mean 'make a reservation' or 'book an appointment'. In Taiwan, you 預約 doctors, restaurants, salons, and services. 預約了 means 'have booked/reserved'.
title: Reservation/Appointment with 預約
meaning: to reserve; to book; to make an appointment
when_to_use: When booking a doctor's appointment
When making a restaurant reservation
When scheduling any service in advance
when_not_to_use: Do not use 訂 (dìng) for appointments — 訂 is for ordering/buying
預約 is specifically for booking time slots and appointments
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 預約 is essential for daily life. Many clinics require 預約, and popular restaurants need reservations. 我預約了 + time is the standard phrase.
word_order_notes: Subject + 預約 + 了 + Time/Place.

korean_contrast: Korean: '예약했어요.' Chinese 預約 corresponds to Korean '예약하다'.

pattern: Subject + 改 + time/plan + 了 / 改到 + new time
explanation_en: Use 改 (gǎi) to mean 'change' or 'modify'. 改時間 (gǎi shíjiān) means 'change the time/reschedule'. 改到 + new time means 'change to (a new time)'. This is essential for managing appointments.
title: Reschedule with 改
meaning: to change; to modify; to reschedule
when_to_use: When you need to reschedule an appointment
When changing plans or arrangements
When modifying a booking
when_not_to_use: Do not confuse 改 (modify/change) with 換 (exchange/swap)
改時間 = change the time, 換時間 = swap time slots with someone
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 改時間 is the standard way to say 'reschedule'. It's direct and clear: 我想改時間 (I want to reschedule).
word_order_notes: Subject + 改 + 了 + Time / 改到 + New Time.
english_contrast: English: 'I need to reschedule.' Chinese: 我需要改時間。(I need change time).
korean_contrast: Korean: '시간을 변경해야 해요.' Chinese 改 corresponds to Korean '변경하다/바꾸다'.

negative_examples.error: This word order is not correct Mandarin.

pattern: Subject + 確認 + 一下 + details / 跟 + person + 確認
explanation_en: Use 確認 (quèrèn) to mean 'confirm'. 確認一下 (quèrèn yīxià) means 'just confirm quickly' — 一下 softens the verb. This is essential for confirming appointments, details, and arrangements in Taiwan.
title: Confirm with 確認
meaning: to confirm; to verify
when_to_use: When double-checking appointment details
When confirming arrangements with someone
When making sure information is correct
when_not_to_use: Do not use 確認 for 'make sure' in a commanding way — add 一下 to soften
確認一下 is much more polite than just 確認
pragmatic_notes: Taiwan speakers frequently use 確認一下 as a soft way to verify information. It's polite and non-confrontational.
word_order_notes: Subject + 確認 + 一下 + Details.
english_contrast: English: 'Let me confirm the time.' Chinese: 我確認一下時間。(I confirm YIXIA time). 一下 softens it.
korean_contrast: Korean: '시간을 확인할게요.' Chinese 確認 corresponds to Korean '확인하다'.

pattern: Subject + 應該 + verb (+ object)
explanation_en: Use 應該 (yīnggāi) to mean 'should' or 'ought to'. It expresses obligation, expectation, or reasonable assumption. It is less strong than 必須 (bìxū, 'must') and is the most common way to give advice in Taiwan.
title: Should/Ought To with 應該
meaning: should; ought to; probably
when_to_use: When giving advice or suggestions
When expressing what is expected or proper
When making a reasonable assumption about something
when_not_to_use: Do not use 應該 for absolute requirements — use 必須 or 一定要
應該 can also mean 'probably' when the context suggests speculation
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 你應該... is the standard way to give friendly advice. It's not pushy — it suggests what would be good to do. 你應該多休息 (You should rest more) is caring, not commanding.
word_order_notes: Subject + 應該 + Verb (+ Object).
english_contrast: English: 'You should rest.' Chinese: 你應該休息。(You should rest).
korean_contrast: Korean: '쉬어야 해요.' Chinese 應該 corresponds to Korean '~해야 하다'.

correct_examples.translation_en: You should rest more.

correct_examples.pinyin: Tā yīnggāi zhīdào zhè jiàn shì.
correct_examples.translation_en: He should know about this matter.

negative_examples.error: This word order is not correct Mandarin.

common_error_patterns: Using yinwei without a clear result clause.

Translating English because too literally and making a fragment.

repair_feedback.meaning: Make sure the listener can hear both cause and result.
repair_feedback.practice: Answer why questions with one short reason and one full yinwei...suoyi... sentence.

pattern: Subject + 需要 + verb/noun (obligation context)
explanation_en: Review of 需要 in obligation contexts. 需要 can express necessity and obligation, similar to 'need to'. In obligation contexts, 需要 is about what is necessary rather than just what is wanted.
title: Need To (Obligation) with 需要
meaning: need to; have to (necessity/obligation)
when_to_use: When expressing what must be done
When stating requirements
When something is necessary, not optional
when_not_to_use: Do not confuse 需要 (necessity) with 想要 (desire)
需要 is about what is needed, not what is wanted
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan workplaces, 你需要... (You need to...) is a standard way to communicate requirements. It's more formal than 要 but less strong than 必須.
word_order_notes: Subject + 需要 + Verb (+ Object).
english_contrast: English: 'You need to fill out this form.' Chinese: 你需要填這張表格。
korean_contrast: Korean: '이 양식을 작성하셔야 해요.' Chinese 需要 corresponds to Korean '~해야 하다/필요하다'.

pattern: Subject + 必須 + verb (+ object)
explanation_en: Use 必須 (bìxū) to mean 'must' or 'have to'. This is the strongest obligation word — it indicates something is mandatory, not optional. Use it for rules, requirements, and non-negotiable necessities.
title: Must/Have To with 必須
meaning: must; have to; necessarily
when_to_use: When something is mandatory or required
When stating rules or regulations
When there is no choice or alternative
when_not_to_use: Do not use 必須 for gentle suggestions — use 應該 instead
必須 is very direct — use carefully in polite conversation
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 必須 is used for official requirements: 你必須帶護照 (You must bring your passport). In casual conversation, 一定要 is more common and slightly softer.
word_order_notes: Subject + 必須 + Verb (+ Object).
english_contrast: English: 'You must bring your passport.' Chinese: 你必須帶護照。(You must bring passport).
korean_contrast: Korean: '반드시 여권을 가져와야 해요.' Chinese 必須 corresponds to Korean '반드시 ~해야 하다'.

pattern: 如果 + condition，就 + result
explanation_en: Use 如果...就... (rúguǒ...jiù...) to express 'if...then...'. 如果 introduces the condition, 就 introduces the result. This is the most common conditional pattern in Taiwan Mandarin.
title: If...Then with 如果...就...
meaning: if... then...
when_to_use: When talking about hypothetical situations
When giving conditional advice or warnings
When making plans that depend on conditions
when_not_to_use: Do not place 就 before 如果 — the condition always comes first
就 is not always required — in casual speech 如果 alone is enough
pragmatic_notes: Taiwan speakers use 如果...就... constantly in daily planning: 如果下雨，我們就不去 (If it rains, we won't go). It's practical and common.
word_order_notes: 如果 + Condition，就 + Result.
english_contrast: English: 'If it rains, I won't go.' Chinese: 如果下雨，我就不去。(If rain, JIU I not go). 就 is like 'then'.
korean_contrast: Korean: '비가 오면 안 갈 거예요.' Chinese 如果...就 corresponds to Korean '~면/으면'.

correct_examples.text: 如果明天下雨，我們就不去公園。
correct_examples.pinyin: Rúguǒ míngtiān xià yǔ, wǒmen jiù bú qù gōngyuán.
correct_examples.translation_en: If it rains tomorrow, we won't go to the park.

correct_examples.pinyin: Rúguǒ nǐ yǒu kòng, wǒmen jiù yīqǐ chī fàn.
correct_examples.translation_en: If you're free, let's eat together.

correct_examples.translation_en: If there's a problem, tell me.

pattern: Suggestion/Statement，不然 + alternative
explanation_en: Use 不然 (bùrán) to mean 'otherwise' or 'or else'. It introduces an alternative or consequence. In Taiwan, it's also commonly used to make suggestions: 不然我們... (How about we... instead?).
title: Otherwise/Or Else with 不然
meaning: otherwise; or else; how about instead
when_to_use: When offering an alternative suggestion
When stating the consequence of not doing something
When pivoting to a different plan
when_not_to_use: Do not confuse 不然 (otherwise) with 但是 (but)
不然 can be used at the start of a suggestion: 不然我們去別的地方 (How about we go somewhere else?)
pragmatic_notes: Taiwan speakers love 不然 for making alternative suggestions. '不然我們去吃火鍋' (How about we go eat hotpot instead?) is a very natural way to change plans.
word_order_notes: Statement，不然 + Alternative.
english_contrast: English: 'Let's go, otherwise we'll be late.' Chinese: 我們走吧，不然會遲到。(We go BA, otherwise will late).
korean_contrast: Korean: '안 그러면 늦을 거야.' Chinese 不然 corresponds to Korean '안 그러면/그렇지 않으면'.

correct_examples.translation_en: Hurry up, otherwise we'll be late.

correct_examples.pinyin: Bùrán wǒmen qù bié de dìfāng?
correct_examples.translation_en: How about we go somewhere else instead?

negative_examples.error: This word order is not correct Mandarin.

common_error_patterns: Using le with every past-time sentence even when the sentence only describes a state.

Forgetting a clear time word when the learner wants to emphasize completed action.

repair_feedback.word_order: Put the time expression before the verb phrase, then put le after the completed action/object.
repair_feedback.meaning: Use le for a bounded completed event, not just because English uses past tense.
repair_feedback.practice: Repair one past-tense English sentence by deciding first: completed event or background state?

pattern: 可能 + statement / Subject + 可能會 + verb
explanation_en: Use 可能 (kěnéng) to mean 'maybe', 'possibly', or 'might'. 可能會 means 'might (do something)'. This is essential for expressing uncertainty and possibility in Taiwan Mandarin.
title: Might/Possibly with 可能
meaning: maybe; possibly; might
when_to_use: When you're not sure about something
When expressing possibility or probability
When giving a tentative answer
when_not_to_use: Do not use 可能 for things you're certain about
可能會 = might (future action), 可能 = maybe (general possibility)
pragmatic_notes: Taiwan speakers use 可能 to stay non-committal and polite. '我可能沒辦法' (I might not be able to) is a softer way to decline than a direct 'no'.
word_order_notes: 可能 + Statement / Subject + 可能會 + Verb.
english_contrast: English: 'I might not be able to go.' Chinese: 我可能不能去。(I maybe not can go).
korean_contrast: Korean: '아마 못 갈 것 같아요.' Chinese 可能 corresponds to Korean '아마/~ㄹ 것 같다'.

correct_examples.translation_en: I might not be able to go.
He might be late.

negative_examples.error: This word order is not correct Mandarin.

用 '可能會' 說未來可能的事

common_error_patterns: Keeping English question word order.
Putting ma in a sentence that already has a question word.
Using shenme when zenme or na li is needed.

repair_feedback.word_order: Keep Chinese statement word order; put the question word in the answer slot.
repair_feedback.meaning: Use ma for yes/no questions, not with shenme/na li/zenme questions.
repair_feedback.practice: Turn three statements into questions without moving the subject and verb.

pattern: 雖然 + clause，可是/但是 + clause
explanation_en: Use 雖然...可是/但是... (suīrán...kěshì/dànshì...) to express 'although...but...'. 雖然 introduces a concession, 可是/但是 introduces the contrasting result. This is the standard contrast pattern in Taiwan Mandarin.
title: Although...But with 雖然...可是...
meaning: although... but...
when_to_use: When acknowledging something but stating a contrasting point
When making a nuanced statement with two sides
When giving balanced opinions
when_not_to_use: Do not use 但/可是 without 雖然 when the contrast is very strong
In English you can't say 'although...but' — in Chinese the pair is standard
pragmatic_notes: Taiwan speakers use 雖然...可是... frequently in conversation. It shows you can see both sides of a situation. 可是 is more casual than 但是 in Taiwan.
word_order_notes: 雖然 + Clause A，可是/但是 + Clause B.
english_contrast: English: 'Although it's expensive, it's delicious.' Chinese: 雖然很貴，可是很好吃。(Although very expensive, but delicious).
korean_contrast: Korean: '비싸지만 맛있어요.' Chinese 雖然...可是 corresponds to Korean '~지만'.

correct_examples.pinyin: Suīrán tā gōngzuò hěn máng, dànshì tā měitiān dōu huì dǎ diànhuà gěi māma.
correct_examples.translation_en: Although he is very busy with work, he calls his mother every day.

negative_examples.error: This word order is not correct Mandarin.

repair_feedback.word_order: Use suiran + concession, danshi/ke shi + contrast.
repair_feedback.meaning: The second clause carries the speaker?s main point.
repair_feedback.practice: Say one sentence about a problem, then contrast it with what you can still do.

pattern: Clause，但是/可是 + contrasting clause
explanation_en: Review of 但是 (dànshì) and 可是 (kěshì) for 'but'. 可是 is slightly more common in Taiwan casual speech. Both connect contrasting clauses. Unlike 雖然...但是, you can use 但是/可是 alone without 雖然.
title: But (Review) with 但是/可是
meaning: but; however
when_to_use: When contrasting two ideas
When adding a counterpoint
In everyday Taiwan conversation
when_not_to_use: Do not start a sentence with 但是/可是 in formal writing
可是 is more casual than 但是
pragmatic_notes: Taiwan speakers prefer 可是 over 但是 in daily conversation. 可是 feels softer and more natural in casual settings.
word_order_notes: Clause A，可是/但是 + Clause B.
english_contrast: English: 'I want to go, but I'm busy.' Chinese: 我想去，可是我很忙。(I want go, but I very busy).
korean_contrast: Korean: '가고 싶은데 바빠요.' Chinese 可是/但是 corresponds to Korean '~ㄴ데/지만'.

pattern: Subject + 還是 + verb / 雖然...，還是...
explanation_en: Use 還是 (háishì) to mean 'still' or 'nevertheless'. When combined with 雖然, it emphasizes that the action continues despite the circumstance. 還是 is different from 還是 (háishì) meaning 'or' (in questions).
title: Still/Nevertheless with 還是
meaning: still; nevertheless; had better
when_to_use: When something continues despite obstacles
When emphasizing persistence
When giving advice: 你還是... (you'd better...)
when_not_to_use: Do not confuse 還是 (still) with 還是 (or) — same characters, different function
還是 + verb = still doing; 還是 + noun = still is/are (with 是 implied)
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 還是 is versatile. 我還是覺得... (I still think...) is common. 你還是去看看吧 (You'd better go check) is friendly advice.
word_order_notes: Subject + 還是 + Verb / 雖然...，Subject + 還是 + Verb.
english_contrast: English: 'I still think it's good.' Chinese: 我還是覺得很好。(I still think very good).
korean_contrast: Korean: '그래도 좋은 것 같아요.' Chinese 還是 corresponds to Korean '그래도/여전히'.

pattern: Verb + 來 / Verb + 去
explanation_en: Use 來 (lái) and 去 (qù) as direction complements after verbs. Verb + 來 indicates movement toward the speaker. Verb + 去 indicates movement away from the speaker. This is fundamental for describing movement in Chinese.
title: Direction Complements 來/去
meaning: toward speaker (來) / away from speaker (去)
when_to_use: When describing movement toward or away from the speaker
When giving directions
When narrating where someone went
when_not_to_use: Do not confuse the direction of 來 (toward speaker) and 去 (away from speaker)
The direction is relative to the SPEAKER, not the subject
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, direction complements are essential for clear communication. 拿來 (bring here) vs 拿去 (take away) is a daily-life distinction.
word_order_notes: Verb + 來 (toward speaker) / Verb + 去 (away from speaker).
english_contrast: English: 'Bring it here' (toward me) vs 'Take it away' (away from me). Chinese: 拿來 vs 拿去.
korean_contrast: Korean: '가져와' (bring) vs '가져가' (take). Chinese 來/去 corresponds to Korean '오다/가다'.

correct_examples.translation_en: Please walk in.

pattern: Verb + 進 + 來/去 / Verb + 出 + 來/去
explanation_en: Use 進 (jìn, 'enter') and 出 (chū, 'exit') as direction complements combined with 來/去. 進來 = come in, 進去 = go in, 出來 = come out, 出去 = go out. These compound direction complements are extremely common.
title: In/Out Complements with 進/出
meaning: in/out direction complements
when_to_use: When describing entering or exiting
When giving instructions about movement
In daily spatial descriptions
when_not_to_use: Do not reverse the order — 進/出 comes before 來/去
進來 = come in (toward speaker), 進去 = go in (away from speaker)
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 進來！(Come in!) and 出去！(Get out!) are direct and common. For politeness, add 請: 請進來 (Please come in).
word_order_notes: Verb + 進 + 來/去 / Verb + 出 + 來/去.
english_contrast: English: 'Come in.' Chinese: 進來。'Go out.' Chinese: 出去。
korean_contrast: Korean: '들어와' (come in) / '나가' (go out). Chinese 進來/出去 corresponds to Korean '들어오다/나가다'.

pattern: Verb + 上 + 來/去 / Verb + 下 + 來/去
explanation_en: Use 上 (shàng, 'up') and 下 (xià, 'down') as direction complements. 上來 = come up, 上去 = go up, 下來 = come down, 下去 = go down. These describe vertical movement.
title: Up/Down Complements with 上/下
meaning: up/down direction complements
when_to_use: When describing vertical movement
When giving directions involving stairs, elevators, etc.
In Taiwan, for getting on/off transportation
when_not_to_use: Do not confuse 上 (up/on) and 下 (down/off) directions
上車 = get on a vehicle, 下車 = get off a vehicle
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 上 and 下 are used for vehicles too: 上車 (get on), 下車 (get off), 上樓 (go upstairs), 下樓 (go downstairs).
word_order_notes: Verb + 上 + 來/去 / Verb + 下 + 來/去.
english_contrast: English: 'Come up' vs 'Go down.' Chinese: 上來 vs 下去.
korean_contrast: Korean: '올라와' vs '내려가'. Chinese 上來/下去 corresponds to Korean '올라오다/내려가다'.

pattern: 帶 + 來 / 帶 + 去
explanation_en: Use 帶 (dài) with direction complements: 帶來 (bring here) and 帶去 (take there). 帶 means 'to bring/carry/take along'. The direction complement indicates movement relative to the speaker.
title: Bring/Take with 帶來/帶去
meaning: bring (toward speaker) / take (away from speaker)
when_to_use: When asking someone to bring something to you
When taking something somewhere
When talking about carrying items
when_not_to_use: Do not confuse 帶 (bring/take/carry) with 拿 (hold/grab)
帶來 = bring to me/us, 帶去 = take to them/there
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 你帶什麼來？(What did you bring?) is a common question when someone visits. 我帶你去 (I'll take you there) is a friendly offer.
word_order_notes: 帶 + Object + 來/去.
english_contrast: English: 'Bring your friend.' Chinese: 帶你的朋友來。(Bring your friend come).
korean_contrast: Korean: '친구를 데려와.' Chinese 帶來 corresponds to Korean '데려오다/가져오다'.

correct_examples.translation_en: Please bring your passport.
I'll take you to the hospital.

pattern: 回 + 來 / 回 + 去
explanation_en: Use 回 (huí) with direction complements: 回來 (come back) and 回去 (go back). 回 means 'to return'. Combined with 來/去, it indicates returning toward or away from the speaker.
title: Return with 回來/回去
meaning: come back (toward speaker) / go back (away from speaker)
when_to_use: When talking about returning home
When asking when someone will come back
When discussing travel and returns
when_not_to_use: Do not confuse 回來 (come back) with 回家 (go home)
回來 = come back HERE, 回去 = go back THERE
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 你什麼時候回來？(When are you coming back?) is a common question. 我要回去了 (I'm going back now) signals departure.
word_order_notes: 回 + 來/去.
english_contrast: English: 'Come back soon.' Chinese: 快點回來。(Quick come back).
korean_contrast: Korean: '빨리 돌아와.' Chinese 回來 corresponds to Korean '돌아오다'.

correct_examples.translation_en: When is he coming back?
I'm going back now.

negative_examples.error: This word order is not correct Mandarin.

transformation_drills: 說 '回來' vs '回去'
問 '你什麼時候回來？'
common_error_patterns: Choosing lai/qu from English perspective instead of speaker location.
Forgetting the direction complement after the main verb.
Using bring/take English one-to-one without checking movement direction.

repair_feedback.word_order: Verb + direction complement shows movement toward or away from the speaker/reference point.
repair_feedback.meaning: First choose the reference point; then choose lai or qu.
repair_feedback.practice: Describe moving one object toward you and one object away from you.

drill_examples.translation_en: When is he coming back?
I'm going back now.

pattern: 拿 + 來 / 拿 + 去
explanation_en: Use 拿 (ná) with direction complements: 拿來 (bring here by holding) and 拿去 (take there by holding). 拿 specifically means 'to hold and carry' — it involves physically picking something up.
title: Fetch/Bring with 拿來/拿去
meaning: bring (holding) / take (holding)
when_to_use: When asking someone to physically bring you something
When moving objects between locations
In daily household and office situations
when_not_to_use: Do not confuse 拿 (hold/carry) with 帶 (bring along/accompany)
拿來/拿去 implies physically holding the object
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 幫我拿一下 (Hold this for me a moment) is common. 拿來給我 (Bring it to me) is a direct request.
word_order_notes: 拿 + Object + 來/去.
english_contrast: English: 'Bring me that book.' Chinese: 把那本書拿來。(BA that book bring here).
korean_contrast: Korean: '그 책을 가져와.' Chinese 拿來 corresponds to Korean '가져오다'.

correct_examples.pinyin: Bǎ nà ge ná lái gěi wǒ.
correct_examples.translation_en: Bring that over to me.
Take the trash out (to throw away).

negative_examples.error: This word order is not correct Mandarin.

transformation_drills: 說 '拿來' vs '拿去'
用 '拿' 說三個動作
common_error_patterns: Forgetting jiu in the result clause after ruguo.
Putting ruguo in both clauses.
Using English if word order and losing the result marker.

repair_feedback.word_order: Use ruguo + condition, jiu + result when the result follows naturally.
repair_feedback.meaning: The first clause sets the condition; the second clause says what will happen.
repair_feedback.practice: Repair two English if sentences into ruguo...jiu... Chinese order.

drill_examples.pinyin: Bǎ nà ge ná lái gěi wǒ.

pattern: Review: Problem Solving Review (Days 61-69)
explanation_en: Consolidation and review of grammar patterns from days 61-69.
title: Problem Solving Review (Days 61-69)
meaning: Review of key patterns from days 61-69.
when_to_use: When reviewing previously learned patterns.
when_not_to_use: Not a new pattern — focus on integration.
pragmatic_notes: Use this session to practice combining multiple patterns naturally in conversation.
word_order_notes: Pay attention to how different patterns interact in longer sentences.
english_contrast: Practice producing Chinese without translating from English.
korean_contrast: Practice producing Chinese without translating from Korean.

repair_feedback.meaning: Use ma for yes/no questions, not with shenme/na li/zenme questions.
repair_feedback.practice: Turn three statements into questions without moving the subject and verb.

pattern: Subject + 在 + company + 上班 / 工作
explanation_en: Use 在 + place + 上班 (shàngbān) to say 'work at [place]'. 上班 specifically means 'to go to work/be at work'. 工作 (gōngzuò) is the general verb for 'work'. This distinction is important in Taiwan.
title: Work At with 在...上班
meaning: to work at (a place); to be employed at
when_to_use: When saying where you work
When asking about someone's workplace
In professional introductions
when_not_to_use: Do not use 工作 without 在 for specifying workplace
上班 = go to work/be at work, 工作 = work (general activity)
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 你在哪裡上班？(Where do you work?) is a standard getting-to-know-you question. 上班 emphasizes the employment relationship.
word_order_notes: Subject + 在 + Place + 上班/工作.
english_contrast: English: 'I work at a tech company.' Chinese: 我在科技公司上班。(I at tech company work).
korean_contrast: Korean: '저는 IT 회사에서 일해요.' Chinese 在...上班 corresponds to Korean '~에서 일하다'.

correct_examples.translation_en: I work at a tech company.
He works at a hospital.
Where do you work?

negative_examples.error: This word order is not correct Mandarin.

transformation_drills: 說你在哪裡上班
問朋友 '你在哪裡上班？'
common_error_patterns: Forgetting jiu in the result clause after ruguo.
Putting ruguo in both clauses.
Using English if word order and losing the result marker.

repair_feedback.word_order: Use ruguo + condition, jiu + result when the result follows naturally.
repair_feedback.meaning: The first clause sets the condition; the second clause says what will happen.
repair_feedback.practice: Repair two English if sentences into ruguo...jiu... Chinese order.

pattern: Subject + 當 + role / Subject + 是 + 當 + role + 的
explanation_en: Use 當 (dāng) to mean 'to serve as' or 'to work as'. 當老師 = to be a teacher, 當醫生 = to be a doctor. 當 is the standard verb for describing one's professional role in Taiwan.
title: Work As with 當
meaning: to work as; to serve as; to be (a role)
when_to_use: When describing your profession or role
When asking what someone does for a living
In career-related conversations
when_not_to_use: Do not use 是 for professional roles in the same way — 我是老師 and 我當老師 are both correct but 當 emphasizes the role/function
當 can also mean 'when' — context distinguishes
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 你當什麼？(What do you do?) is a casual way to ask about someone's job. 我當工程師 (I work as an engineer) is a common answer.
word_order_notes: Subject + 當 + Role.
english_contrast: English: 'She works as a nurse.' Chinese: 她當護士。(She serve-as nurse).
korean_contrast: Korean: '간호사로 일해요.' Chinese 當 corresponds to Korean '~로 일하다'.

correct_examples.translation_en: He works as a teacher.
My older sister works as a nurse.

negative_examples.error: This word order is not correct Mandarin.

transformation_drills: 用 '當' 說三種職業
問 '你做什麼工作？' 兩種問法

Putting ma in a sentence that already has a question word.
Using shenme when zenme or na li is needed.

repair_feedback.word_order: Keep Chinese statement word order; put the question word in the answer slot.

pattern: Subject + 忙不忙？/ Subject + 很忙 / Subject + 不太忙
explanation_en: Use 忙 (máng) to describe being busy. 忙不忙 (máng bù máng) is the A-not-A question form: 'Are you busy?' This is a very common small-talk pattern in Taiwan workplaces.
title: Busy with 忙
meaning: busy; occupied
when_to_use: When asking about someone's workload
When explaining why you can't do something
In workplace small talk
when_not_to_use: Do not say 你是忙 — adjectives don't need 是
忙不忙 is the standard question form
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 你最近忙不忙？(Have you been busy lately?) is extremely common small talk. It shows care about the person's wellbeing.
word_order_notes: Subject + 忙不忙？/ Subject + 很忙 / Subject + 不忙.
english_contrast: English: 'Are you busy?' Chinese: 你忙不忙？(You busy-not-busy?).
korean_contrast: Korean: '바빠요?' Chinese 忙不忙 corresponds to Korean '바쁘다'.

correct_examples.translation_en: Have you been busy lately?
He is very busy with work.

negative_examples.error: This word order is not correct Mandarin.

transformation_drills: 問 '你忙不忙？'
回答 '很忙' / '不太忙'
common_error_patterns: Using bei for neutral English passive sentences where Chinese would use active voice.
Forgetting the agent or result after bei.
Using bei for positive routine events too often.

repair_feedback.word_order: Use object + bei + agent + verb when the affected object is the focus.
repair_feedback.meaning: Bei often sounds negative or affected; use it carefully.
repair_feedback.practice: Decide if an event should be active, ba, or bei before translating.

drill_examples.translation_en: Have you been busy lately?
He is very busy with work.

pattern: 快要 + verb + 了 / 就要 + verb + 了
explanation_en: Use 快要...了 (kuài yào...le) or 就要...了 (jiù yào...le) to mean 'about to' or 'almost'. 快要 is more colloquial, 就要 is slightly more formal. Both indicate imminence.
title: About To with 快要...了
meaning: about to; almost; soon
when_to_use: When something is about to happen
When you're almost finished with something
When indicating urgency or imminence
when_not_to_use: Do not forget 了 at the end — it's essential to the pattern
快要...了 and 就要...了 both need 了
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 快好了 (almost ready/done) is a very common phrase. 快要下班了 (almost time to get off work) is heard daily.
word_order_notes: 快要 + Verb + 了 / 就要 + Verb + 了.
english_contrast: English: 'I'm almost done.' Chinese: 我快做好了。(I almost do-finish LE).
korean_contrast: Korean: '거의 다 했어요.' Chinese 快要...了 corresponds to Korean '거의 ~했다'.

correct_examples.translation_en: I'm almost done.
It's almost time to get off work.
The meeting is almost over.

negative_examples.error: This word order is not correct Mandarin.

transformation_drills: 用 '快要...了' 說三個快完成的事
用 '就要...了' 替代
common_error_patterns: Using le with every past-time sentence even when the sentence only describes a state.
Putting le immediately after the subject instead of after the completed verb phrase.
Forgetting a clear time word when the learner wants to emphasize completed action.

repair_feedback.word_order: Put the time expression before the verb phrase, then put le after the completed action/object.
repair_feedback.meaning: Use le for a bounded completed event, not just because English uses past tense.
repair_feedback.practice: Repair one past-tense English sentence by deciding first: completed event or background state?

drill_examples.translation_en: I'm almost done.
It's almost time to get off work.
The meeting is almost over.

pattern: Subject + 在 + 開會 / Subject + 正在 + 開會
explanation_en: Use 在開會 (zài kāihuì) to say 'in a meeting'. 開會 (kāihuì) means 'to have/hold a meeting'. 在 + 開會 indicates the meeting is in progress. This is essential workplace vocabulary.
title: In a Meeting with 在開會
meaning: in a meeting; having a meeting
when_to_use: When you're in a meeting and can't talk
When explaining unavailability at work
When scheduling around meetings
when_not_to_use: Do not say 我在會議 — use 我在開會 (I'm in a meeting)
開會 emphasizes the action of having the meeting
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan offices, 我在開會 (I'm in a meeting) is the standard way to say you're unavailable. It's direct and clear.
word_order_notes: Subject + 在 + 開會.
english_contrast: English: 'I'm in a meeting.' Chinese: 我在開會。(I ZAI open-meeting).
korean_contrast: Korean: '회의 중이에요.' Chinese 在開會 corresponds to Korean '회의 중이다'.

correct_examples.text: 他在開會，不方便接電話。
correct_examples.pinyin: Tā zài kāihuì, bù fāngbiàn jiē diànhuà.
correct_examples.translation_en: He's in a meeting and can't take a call right now.

correct_examples.pinyin: Wǒmen xiàwǔ sān diǎn yào kāihuì.
correct_examples.translation_en: We have a meeting at 3 PM.

negative_examples.error: This word order is not correct Mandarin.

common_error_patterns: Using zai with stative verbs like need, like, or know.
Forgetting that zai marks an action happening right now.
Putting zai after the verb.

repair_feedback.word_order: Put zai or zhengzai before the action verb.
repair_feedback.meaning: Use progressive markers only for ongoing actions, not states or preferences.
