pattern: Subject + 還沒 + verb (+ 呢)
explanation_en: Use 還沒 (hái méi) to mean 'not yet'. It indicates that something expected hasn't happened yet. Adding 呢 (ne) at the end makes it more conversational. This is extremely common in Taiwan daily speech.
title: Not Yet with 還沒
meaning: not yet; still haven't
when_to_use: When something expected hasn't happened yet
When you're still working on something
When asking if something is done yet
when_not_to_use: Do not confuse 還沒 (not yet) with 沒有 (didn't/haven't)
還沒 implies expectation that it WILL happen
pragmatic_notes: Taiwan speakers use 還沒 constantly: 還沒好 (not ready yet), 還沒吃完 (haven't finished eating yet), 還沒到 (haven't arrived yet).
word_order_notes: Subject + 還沒 + Verb (+ 呢).
english_contrast: English: 'I haven't finished yet.' Chinese: 我還沒做完。(I still not do-finish).
korean_contrast: Korean: '아직 안 끝났어요.' Chinese 還沒 corresponds to Korean '아직 안 ~했다'.

correct_examples.translation_en: I haven't finished yet.
He hasn't come yet.

negative_examples.error: This word order is not correct Mandarin.

pattern: Subject + 覺得 + statement
explanation_en: Use 覺得 (juéde) to express 'think/feel'. This is the most common way to give an opinion in Taiwan Mandarin. 我覺得... (I think/feel...) is softer and more personal than 我認為 (I believe/opine).
title: I Think/Feel with 覺得
meaning: to think; to feel; to be of the opinion that
when_to_use: When giving a personal opinion
When expressing how you feel about something
When softening a statement to be less direct
when_not_to_use: Do not use 覺得 for factual knowledge — use 知道 (zhīdào, know)
覺得 is subjective opinion, 知道 is factual knowledge
pragmatic_notes: Taiwan speakers use 我覺得 constantly. It's a cultural habit to soften opinions by framing them as personal feelings rather than absolute truths. 我覺得不錯 (I think it's not bad) is more polite than 很好 (it's great).
word_order_notes: Subject + 覺得 + Statement.
english_contrast: English: 'I think it's good.' Chinese: 我覺得很好。(I feel very good).
korean_contrast: Korean: '좋은 것 같아요.' Chinese 覺得 corresponds to Korean '~ㄴ 것 같다'.

correct_examples.pinyin: Wǒ juéde zhè jiā cāntīng búcuò.
correct_examples.translation_en: I think this restaurant is not bad.

pattern: Subject + 認為 + statement
explanation_en: Use 認為 (rènwéi) to express 'believe/opine/consider'. It is more formal than 覺得 and is used for considered opinions, beliefs, and positions. In Taiwan, 認為 is common in workplace discussions and more serious conversations.
title: I Believe/Opine with 認為
meaning: to believe; to think; to consider; to opine
when_to_use: When expressing a considered opinion
In workplace or formal discussions
When stating a position or belief
when_not_to_use: Do not use 認為 for casual feelings — use 覺得 instead
認為 carries more weight than 覺得
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan business settings, 我認為... signals a formal opinion. In casual settings, 我覺得... is more natural. The distinction helps navigate formality levels.
word_order_notes: Subject + 認為 + Statement.
english_contrast: English: 'I believe this plan will work.' Chinese: 我認為這個計畫會成功。(I believe this plan will succeed).
korean_contrast: Korean: '저는 이 계획이 성공할 거라고 생각합니다.' Chinese 認為 corresponds to formal Korean '~라고 생각합니다'.

pattern: Subject + 不一定 + statement
explanation_en: Use 不一定 (bù yīdìng) to mean 'not necessarily' or 'not always'. It expresses that something is not absolute. This is a very useful phrase for nuanced opinions in Taiwan Mandarin.
title: Not Necessarily with 不一定
meaning: not necessarily; not always; not for sure
when_to_use: When something is not always true
When expressing uncertainty or nuance
When politely disagreeing
when_not_to_use: Do not confuse 不一定 (not necessarily) with 一定不 (definitely not)
不一定 = maybe not, 一定不 = definitely not — very different meanings
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 不一定 is a polite way to express doubt or disagreement without being confrontational. '貴的東西不一定好吃' (Expensive things aren't necessarily delicious).
word_order_notes: Subject + 不一定 + Statement.
english_contrast: English: 'Expensive doesn't necessarily mean good.' Chinese: 貴的不一定好。(Expensive DE not-necessarily good).
korean_contrast: Korean: '비싼 게 꼭 좋은 건 아니에요.' Chinese 不一定 corresponds to Korean '꼭 ~한 것은 아니다'.

correct_examples.pinyin: Guì de dōngxi bù yīdìng hǎochī.
correct_examples.translation_en: Expensive things are not necessarily delicious.
He might not come (it's not certain).

pattern: 畢竟 + statement
explanation_en: Use 畢竟 (bìjìng) to mean 'after all' or 'in the final analysis'. It introduces a fundamental reason or fact that supports your argument. It's a sophisticated connector for intermediate-level discussion.
title: After All with 畢竟
meaning: after all; in the end; ultimately
when_to_use: When giving a fundamental reason for something
When acknowledging an underlying truth
In discussions and arguments
when_not_to_use: Do not use 畢竟 for minor, non-fundamental points
畢竟 introduces the CORE reason, not just any reason
pragmatic_notes: Taiwan speakers use 畢竟 in discussions to ground their arguments: 畢竟我們是朋友 (After all, we are friends). It adds depth to reasoning.
word_order_notes: 畢竟 + Statement.
english_contrast: English: 'After all, we're friends.' Chinese: 畢竟我們是朋友。(After-all we are friends).
korean_contrast: Korean: '어차피 우리는 친구잖아요.' Chinese 畢竟 corresponds to Korean '어차피/결국'.

correct_examples.text: 畢竟我們是朋友，應該互相幫忙。
correct_examples.pinyin: Bìjìng wǒmen shì péngyou, yīnggāi hùxiāng bāngmáng.
correct_examples.translation_en: After all, we're friends; we should help each other.

correct_examples.text: 畢竟他剛來，還不太熟悉環境。
correct_examples.pinyin: Bìjìng tā gāng lái, hái bú tài shúxī huánjìng.
correct_examples.translation_en: After all, he just arrived and isn't very familiar with the environment yet.

correct_examples.text: 畢竟中文不是我的母語，所以有時候會犯錯。
correct_examples.pinyin: Bìjìng Zhōngwén bú shì wǒ de mǔyǔ, suǒyǐ yǒu shíhou huì fàncuò.

pattern: 例如 + example / 比如 + example
explanation_en: Use 例如 (lìrú) or 比如 (bǐrú) to mean 'for example'. 比如 is more common in casual Taiwan speech. Both are essential for giving examples and supporting arguments.
title: For Example with 例如/比如
meaning: for example; for instance; such as
when_to_use: When giving an example to support a point
When clarifying with a specific instance
In both casual and formal discussions
when_not_to_use: Do not confuse 例如/比如 (for example) with 如果 (if)
比如 is more casual and common in Taiwan speech
pragmatic_notes: Taiwan speakers prefer 比如 in daily conversation. 例如 is more common in writing and formal presentations. Both are correct.
word_order_notes: 例如/比如 + Example.
english_contrast: English: 'For example, in Taiwan...' Chinese: 比如在台灣...(For-example in Taiwan...).
korean_contrast: Korean: '예를 들어 대만에서는...' Chinese 例如/比如 corresponds to Korean '예를 들어'.

pattern: Statement，所以 + conclusion
explanation_en: Use 所以 (suǒyǐ) to mean 'so' or 'therefore'. It introduces a conclusion or result. This is one of the most frequently used connectors in Taiwan Mandarin for reasoning and argumentation.
title: So/Therefore with 所以
meaning: so; therefore; thus
when_to_use: When drawing a conclusion from previous statements
When explaining reasoning
When summarizing an argument
when_not_to_use: Do not use 所以 at the beginning of a completely new topic
所以 should logically follow from what was just said
pragmatic_notes: Taiwan speakers use 所以 constantly. It's one of the most useful connectors. 所以呢？(So?) is a common way to ask someone to get to the point.
word_order_notes: Statement，所以 + Conclusion.
english_contrast: English: 'It's raining, so I won't go.' Chinese: 下雨了，所以我不去。(Rain LE, so I not go).
korean_contrast: Korean: '비가 와서 안 가요.' Chinese 所以 corresponds to Korean '~서/그래서'.

pattern: 本來 + plan/thought，可是後來 + change，結果 + outcome
explanation_en: Use 本來...後來...結果... (běnlái...hòulái...jiéguǒ...) to tell a story with a change of plans. 本來 introduces the original plan, 後來 introduces what changed, and 結果 introduces the final outcome. This is the classic Chinese storytelling framework.
title: Originally...Later...Result with 本來...後來...結果
meaning: originally... later... in the end...
when_to_use: When telling a story with a change of plans
When narrating an unexpected outcome
When explaining why things turned out differently
when_not_to_use: Do not use 本來 for future plans — it refers to original intentions that may have changed
The full 本來...後來...結果 pattern is for longer narratives
pragmatic_notes: Taiwan speakers love this storytelling pattern. It's how you tell engaging stories about what almost happened but didn't. The three-part structure is very natural in Chinese narrative.
word_order_notes: 本來 + Original Plan，可是後來 + What Changed，結果 + Final Outcome.
english_contrast: English: 'I was going to go, but then it rained, so I ended up staying home.' Chinese: 我本來要去，可是後來下雨了，結果我待在家裡。
korean_contrast: Korean: '원래 가려고 했는데 비가 와서 결국 집에 있었어요.' Chinese 本來...後來...結果 corresponds to Korean '원래...는데...결국'.

correct_examples.pinyin: Tā běnlái dǎsuàn qù gōngyuán sànbù, kěshì hòulái túrán xià yǔ le, jiéguǒ tā dāi zài jiā lǐ.
correct_examples.translation_en: He originally planned to go for a walk in the park, but later it suddenly rained, so he ended up staying at home.

correct_examples.text: 我本來想吃牛肉麵，可是後來發現那家餐廳已經關門了，結果我去吃水餃。

correct_examples.translation_en: I originally wanted to eat beef noodles, but later I found out that restaurant was already closed, so I went to eat dumplings instead.

pattern: 因為 + reason，結果 + unexpected outcome
explanation_en: Use 因為...結果... (yīnwèi...jiéguǒ...) to tell a cause-and-effect story where the result was somewhat unexpected. 因為 introduces the cause, 結果 introduces the surprising or noteworthy outcome.
title: Because...It Turned Out with 因為...結果
meaning: because... it turned out that...
when_to_use: When explaining an unexpected outcome
When the cause seems small but the result was significant
When telling an anecdote with a twist
when_not_to_use: Do not use 結果 for expected, logical outcomes — it often implies surprise
因為...結果 can sometimes be shortened to just 結果 in casual speech
pragmatic_notes: Taiwan speakers use 結果 to add drama to stories. The word carries a slight sense of 'and guess what happened?' which makes narratives more engaging.
word_order_notes: 因為 + Reason，結果 + Outcome.
english_contrast: English: 'Because I was curious, I ended up trying it.' Chinese: 因為好奇，結果我試了。(Because curious, result I tried).
korean_contrast: Korean: '궁금해서 결국 해봤어요.' Chinese 結果 corresponds to Korean '결국'.

correct_examples.pinyin: Wǒ běnlái juéde jīntiān xīnqíng bù hǎo, kěshì hòulái yù dào yī ge lǎo péngyou, jiéguǒ hěn kāixīn.
correct_examples.translation_en: I originally felt in a bad mood today, but later I ran into an old friend, and in the end I was very happy.

pattern: Event，Subject + 覺得 + feeling / Event，Subject + 很 + feeling adjective
explanation_en: Use this pattern to express feelings about an event. After narrating what happened, add your emotional response. This makes stories personal and relatable in Taiwan Mandarin.
title: Expressing Feelings About Events
meaning: describing emotional reactions to experiences
when_to_use: When adding emotional depth to a story
When sharing how an experience made you feel
When connecting with listeners through shared feelings
when_not_to_use: Do not use 是 before feelings — Chinese emotions use 很 or 覺得
Some emotions use 很 (我很開心), others use 覺得 (我覺得很感動)
pragmatic_notes: Taiwan culture values emotional authenticity in storytelling. Sharing how you felt makes stories more engaging and builds connection with listeners.
word_order_notes: Event，Subject + 覺得 + Feeling / Event，Subject + 很 + Feeling Adjective.
english_contrast: English: 'It made me very happy.' Chinese: 我覺得很開心。(I feel very happy).
korean_contrast: Korean: '정말 기뻤어요.' Chinese emotional expressions correspond to Korean emotional adjectives/adverbs.

pattern: Statement，然後呢？/ Clause 1，然後 + clause 2 + 呢
explanation_en: Use 然後呢 (ránhòu ne) to ask 'and then?' or 'what happened next?' Adding 呢 makes it a question that prompts the speaker to continue. This is essential for active listening and showing engagement in Taiwan conversations.
title: And Then? with 然後呢
meaning: and then?; what happened next?
when_to_use: When asking someone to continue their story
When showing interest and engagement
When prompting for more details
when_not_to_use: Do not overuse 然後呢 — it can sound impatient if repeated too much
呢 adds the questioning tone — without it, 然後 just means 'and then'
pragmatic_notes: Taiwan listeners use 然後呢？ to show they're actively listening. It's a friendly way to keep someone talking. Combined with nodding, it's the standard active-listening response.
word_order_notes: Statement，然後呢？
english_contrast: English: 'And then what happened?' Chinese: 然後呢？(Then NE?). 呢 makes it a question.
korean_contrast: Korean: '그리고 나서요?' Chinese 然後呢 corresponds to Korean '그리고요?/그 다음에요?'.

repair_feedback.meaning: First choose the reference point; then choose lai or qu.

pattern: 後來 + clause / Clause 1，後來 + clause 2
explanation_en: Use 後來 (hòulái) to mean 'later' or 'afterwards'. It introduces what happened next in a narrative sequence. Unlike 然後 (and then, immediate next), 後來 can refer to a later point in time, not necessarily the immediate next action.
title: Later/Afterwards with 後來
meaning: later; afterwards; subsequently
when_to_use: When narrating a sequence of past events
When there is a time gap between events
When the later event is more significant
when_not_to_use: Do not confuse 後來 (later, past) with 以後 (after, future)
後來 is specifically for past narratives, 以後 can be future
pragmatic_notes: Taiwan speakers use 後來 in storytelling to mark a shift in time. '後來呢？' (What happened later?) is another common story-prompting question.
word_order_notes: 後來 + Clause.
english_contrast: English: 'Later, I found out the truth.' Chinese: 後來我發現了真相。(Later I discovered truth).
korean_contrast: Korean: '나중에 진실을 알게 됐어요.' Chinese 後來 corresponds to Korean '나중에/후에'.

correct_examples.text: 後來我才知道他已經離開了。
correct_examples.pinyin: Hòulái wǒ cái zhīdào tā yǐjīng líkāi le.
correct_examples.translation_en: Only later did I find out that he had already left.

correct_examples.pinyin: Hòulái fāshēng le hěn duō shìqing.
correct_examples.translation_en: Later, many things happened.

negative_examples.error: This word order is not correct Mandarin.

transformation_drills: 用 '後來' 說故事的時間轉折
比較 '後來' 和 '然後'
production_drills: 說一個後來才知道的事
common_error_patterns: Using le with every past-time sentence even when the sentence only describes a state.
Putting le immediately after the subject instead of after the completed verb phrase.
Forgetting a clear time word when the learner wants to emphasize completed action.

repair_feedback.word_order: Put the time expression before the verb phrase, then put le after the completed action/object.
repair_feedback.meaning: Use le for a bounded completed event, not just because English uses past tense.
repair_feedback.practice: Repair one past-tense English sentence by deciding first: completed event or background state?

drill_examples.text: 後來我才知道他已經離開了。

drill_examples.pinyin: Hòulái fāshēng le hěn duō shìqing.

pattern: 你的意思是 + interpretation？/ 你是說 + interpretation？
explanation_en: Use 你的意思是... (nǐ de yìsi shì...) to mean 'Do you mean...?' or 'Are you saying...?'. This is the standard way to check understanding in Taiwan conversations. It shows you're listening carefully and want to make sure you understood correctly.
title: Do You Mean with 你的意思是
meaning: Do you mean...?; Are you saying...?
when_to_use: When checking your understanding of what someone said
When clarifying ambiguous statements
When showing active listening
when_not_to_use: Do not use this pattern to challenge someone — it's for genuine clarification
你是說... is the shorter, more casual version
pragmatic_notes: Taiwan speakers use 你的意思是... frequently to ensure clear communication. It's polite and shows you value understanding correctly. 你是說... is more casual.
word_order_notes: 你的意思是 + Interpretation？
english_contrast: English: 'Do you mean you're not coming?' Chinese: 你的意思是你不來嗎？(Your meaning is you not come MA?).
korean_contrast: Korean: '그 말은 안 온다는 뜻이에요?' Chinese 你的意思是 corresponds to Korean '그 말은 ~라는 뜻이에요?'.

pattern: 那個... + statement (conversation filler)
explanation_en: Use 那個 (nèige/nàge) as a conversation filler, similar to 'um' or 'uh' in English. In Taiwan casual speech, 那個 is extremely common as a hesitation marker while thinking of what to say next. It's an essential part of natural-sounding Chinese.
title: Conversation Filler 那個
meaning: um; uh; you know (conversation filler)
when_to_use: When pausing to think during conversation
When hesitating or searching for the right word
To sound more natural and less rehearsed
when_not_to_use: Do not overuse 那個 in formal presentations — it can sound unprofessional
In casual conversation, moderate use is perfectly natural
pragmatic_notes: Taiwan speakers use 那個 as a filler constantly. Pronounced 'nèige' in casual speech, it's the most common hesitation marker. Using it appropriately makes your Chinese sound much more natural.
word_order_notes: 那個... + Statement.
english_contrast: English: 'Um, I think...' Chinese: 那個...我覺得...(That-one... I think...).
korean_contrast: Korean: '저... 제 생각에는...' Chinese 那個 as filler corresponds to Korean '저.../그...'.

pattern: 對對對！+ follow-up / 對對對，+ agreement
explanation_en: Use 對對對 (duì duì duì) to enthusiastically agree with someone. Repeating 對 three times is a very Taiwan way to show strong agreement. It's warm, friendly, and shows you're really listening.
title: Enthusiastic Agreement with 對對對
meaning: Yes yes yes!; Exactly!; That's right!
when_to_use: When strongly agreeing with someone
When showing enthusiasm about a shared opinion
When acknowledging someone's point emphatically
when_not_to_use: Do not use 對對對 sarcastically — it's genuinely enthusiastic in Taiwan
Two 對對 is calmer, three 對對對 is very enthusiastic
pragmatic_notes: Taiwan speakers often say 對對對 when they really connect with what someone said. It's a warmth marker in conversation. Combined with nodding, it's a strong positive signal.
word_order_notes: 對對對 + Follow-up statement.
english_contrast: English: 'Yes, exactly! I think so too.' Chinese: 對對對！我也這麼覺得。(Yes-yes-yes! I also this-way think).
korean_contrast: Korean: '맞아맞아! 나도 그렇게 생각해.' Chinese 對對對 corresponds to Korean '맞아맞아!'.

pattern: 我是說 + clarification
explanation_en: Use 我是說 (wǒ shì shuō) to mean 'I mean...' or 'What I'm saying is...'. This is the standard way to clarify or rephrase what you just said in Taiwan Mandarin. It's used when you realize you weren't clear the first time.
title: I Mean with 我是說
meaning: I mean...; What I'm saying is...
when_to_use: When clarifying or rephrasing what you just said
When you realize the listener didn't understand
When correcting a misunderstanding
when_not_to_use: Do not use 我是說 to completely change what you said — it's for clarification, not contradiction
The 是 adds emphasis — it's 'what I MEAN is'
pragmatic_notes: Taiwan speakers use 我是說 frequently in conversation. It's a natural way to self-correct or clarify without losing face. '我是說...不是...' (I mean... not...) is the full correction pattern.
word_order_notes: 我是說 + Clarification.
english_contrast: English: 'I mean, it's not that expensive.' Chinese: 我是說，沒有那麼貴。(I SHI say, not that expensive).
korean_contrast: Korean: '제 말은, 그렇게 비싸지 않다는 거예요.' Chinese 我是說 corresponds to Korean '제 말은...'.

pattern: 請再說一次。/ 可以再說一次嗎？
explanation_en: Use 請再說一次 (qǐng zài shuō yī cì) to ask someone to repeat what they said. This is the most essential repair strategy in Chinese — when you don't understand, this is what you say. It's polite and clear.
title: Please Say It Again with 請再說一次
meaning: Please say that again.; Could you repeat that?
when_to_use: When you didn't hear or understand what someone said
When you need someone to repeat something
As a polite communication repair strategy
when_not_to_use: Do not just say 什麼？(What?) — it can sound rude
請再說一次 is polite; 不好意思，可以再說一次嗎？ is extra polite
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 不好意思，可以再說一次嗎？ is the gold standard for asking someone to repeat. It combines the polite opener 不好意思 with the request pattern 可以...嗎.
word_order_notes: 請 + 再 + 說 + 一次 / 可以 + 再 + 說 + 一次 + 嗎？
english_contrast: English: 'Could you say that again?' Chinese: 可以再說一次嗎？(Can again say one-time MA?).
korean_contrast: Korean: '다시 한 번 말씀해 주시겠어요?' Chinese 再說一次 corresponds to Korean '다시 한 번 말하다'.

pattern: 請 + 慢一點 + 說 / 可以 + 說 + 慢一點 + 嗎？
explanation_en: Use 慢一點 (màn yīdiǎn) to ask someone to slow down. 請說慢一點 (qǐng shuō màn yīdiǎn) means 'please speak more slowly'. This is another essential repair strategy for language learners in Taiwan.
title: Speak Slower with 慢一點
meaning: more slowly; a bit slower
when_to_use: When someone is speaking too fast for you to understand
When you need more time to process what's being said
As a polite request in any conversation
when_not_to_use: Do not just say 太慢了！(Too slow!) — that sounds critical
慢一點 is a polite request; 太快了 (too fast) is a statement of fact
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 可以說慢一點嗎？(Can you speak a bit slower?) is a very common and perfectly acceptable request. Most Taiwanese speakers are happy to accommodate.
word_order_notes: 請 + 說 + 慢一點 / 可以 + 說 + 慢一點 + 嗎？

korean_contrast: Korean: '좀 천천히 말씀해 주시겠어요?' Chinese 慢一點 corresponds to Korean '좀 천천히'.

pattern: 是不是 + meaning？/ ...是不是 + 這個意思？
explanation_en: Use 是不是 (shì bú shì) to confirm understanding: 'Is it...?' or 'Does it mean...?'. This is the A-not-A confirmation pattern. It's a very natural way to check if you understood correctly in Taiwan Mandarin.
title: Is It That with 是不是
meaning: is it...?; does it mean...? (confirmation pattern)
when_to_use: When checking if your understanding is correct
When confirming an interpretation
When you want a yes/no confirmation
when_not_to_use: Do not use 是不是 for open-ended questions — it expects yes/no
是不是 can also be used as a tag: ..., 是不是？(..., right?)
pragmatic_notes: Taiwan speakers use 是不是 constantly to confirm understanding. '你的意思是...是不是？' (You mean..., right?) is a complete clarification pattern.
word_order_notes: 是不是 + Interpretation？/ Statement + 是不是？
english_contrast: English: 'You mean it's free, right?' Chinese: 你的意思是免費的，是不是？(Your meaning is free DE, is-not-is?).
korean_contrast: Korean: '무료라는 거죠?' Chinese 是不是 corresponds to Korean '~죠?/~는 거죠?'.

pattern: 請 + verb / 請勿 + verb
explanation_en: Use 請 (qǐng) on signs and notices to mean 'please'. 請勿 (qǐng wù) means 'please do not'. This is the standard way polite instructions are written on signs throughout Taiwan.
title: Please (on signs) with 請
meaning: please (formal/written instruction)
when_to_use: When reading signs and notices in Taiwan
When writing formal instructions
When understanding public announcements
when_not_to_use: Do not confuse 請勿 (please don't) with 不要 (don't — more direct)
請勿 is formal written style; 不要 is spoken/casual
pragmatic_notes: Taiwan is full of signs with 請: 請排隊 (Please line up), 請勿吸煙 (Please don't smoke), 請隨手關門 (Please close the door behind you). Understanding these is practical for daily life.
word_order_notes: 請 + Verb / 請勿 + Verb.
english_contrast: English: 'Please do not smoke.' Chinese: 請勿吸煙。(Please don't smoke).
korean_contrast: Korean: '담배를 피우지 마십시오.' Chinese 請勿 corresponds to Korean '~하지 마십시오'.

pattern: 不可 + verb / 禁止 + verb
explanation_en: Use 不可 (bù kě) or 禁止 (jìnzhǐ) on signs to mean 'prohibited' or 'not allowed'. 禁止 is stronger and more official. Both are common on Taiwan signs and notices.
title: Prohibited with 不可/禁止
meaning: prohibited; not allowed; forbidden
when_to_use: When reading prohibition signs
When understanding rules and regulations
In formal and official contexts
when_not_to_use: Do not use 不可 in casual speech — it sounds like a sign
禁止 is very strong — it means strictly forbidden
pragmatic_notes: Taiwan signs commonly use 禁止: 禁止停車 (No parking), 禁止進入 (No entry), 禁止拍照 (No photography). These are important for daily navigation.
word_order_notes: 不可 + Verb / 禁止 + Verb.

korean_contrast: Korean: '주차 금지.' Chinese 禁止 corresponds to Korean '금지'.

pattern: Notice style: short imperative / 請 + verb / 禁止 + verb
explanation_en: Understanding Taiwan sign language: Signs use short, direct language. Common patterns include 請 + verb (polite request), 禁止 + verb (prohibition), and direct imperatives. Recognizing these patterns helps navigate daily life in Taiwan.
title: Taiwan Sign Language Patterns
meaning: Understanding written notices and signs in Taiwan
when_to_use: When reading signs in public spaces
When understanding official notices
When navigating Taiwan cities
when_not_to_use: Sign language is more concise than spoken Chinese
Don't expect full sentences — signs are telegraphic
pragmatic_notes: Taiwan has bilingual signs (Chinese and English) in many places. The Chinese is often more detailed than the English. Learning to read common sign patterns is very practical.
word_order_notes: 請 + Verb (polite) / 禁止 + Verb (prohibition) / Direct verb (instruction).
english_contrast: English signs vs Chinese signs: Chinese often uses 請 (please) where English uses direct imperatives.
korean_contrast: Korean signs also use '~주세요' and '금지'. Chinese sign patterns are structurally similar to Korean.

pattern: 喂？+ greeting / 喂，你好
explanation_en: Use 喂 (wéi) to answer the phone in Chinese. It's the standard phone greeting, equivalent to 'Hello?' on the phone. In Taiwan, 喂？with a rising tone is the universal way to start a phone conversation.
title: Phone Hello with 喂
meaning: Hello? (phone greeting)
when_to_use: When answering the phone
When starting a phone conversation
When checking if the other person is still on the line
when_not_to_use: Do not use 喂 to greet someone in person — it's for phone calls only
喂 can sound rude in person but is perfectly normal on the phone
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, the standard phone answer is 喂？(rising tone). Followed by 你好 (nǐ hǎo) in more formal situations. 喂，你好 is the complete polite phone greeting.

pattern: 請問 + name/title + 在嗎？
explanation_en: Use 請問...在嗎？(qǐngwèn...zài ma?) to ask 'Is [name] there?' on the phone. This is the standard polite way to ask for someone on a phone call in Taiwan.

when_to_use: When calling and asking for a specific person
In formal phone calls to offices or businesses
When you don't know if the person is available
when_not_to_use: Do not just say 他在嗎？without 請問 — it sounds abrupt
請問 adds the necessary politeness for phone calls
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan, 請問 + name + 在嗎？is the gold standard for phone etiquette. It's polite and clear. For businesses, 請問 + title + 在嗎？is appropriate.

pattern: 我想找 + person / 我想要 + verb (on phone)
explanation_en: Use 我想找... (wǒ xiǎng zhǎo...) on the phone to mean 'I'm looking for...' or 'I'd like to speak with...'. This is the standard way to state who you want to talk to. 找 (zhǎo) literally means 'find' but on the phone it means 'speak with'.

meaning: I'd like to speak with...; I'm looking for...
when_to_use: When you want to talk to a specific person
When calling a business and need a department
When stating the purpose of your call
when_not_to_use: Do not use 想跟...說話 (want to talk with) — 找 is the idiomatic phone word
找 on the phone = 'looking for / wanting to speak with'
pragmatic_notes: In Taiwan phone calls, 我想找... is the natural way to say who you want. '我想找王先生' (I'd like to speak with Mr. Wang) is standard and polite.

pattern: Message style: Short form / Omitting subject / 知道了 / 收到了
explanation_en: Chinese text messages and LINE messages often use shortened forms. Subjects are frequently dropped, particles are omitted, and responses are telegraphic. 知道了 (zhīdào le, 'noted/got it') and 收到了 (shōudào le, 'received') are common message responses.
title: Message-Style Chinese
meaning: Short-form written Chinese for messages and texts
when_to_use: When reading or writing text messages in Chinese
When using LINE or other messaging apps in Taiwan
When understanding informal written communication
when_not_to_use: Message style is casual — don't use it in formal emails
Dropping the subject is common and natural in messages
pragmatic_notes: LINE is the dominant messaging app in Taiwan. Understanding message-style Chinese is essential for daily communication with Taiwanese friends and colleagues.
word_order_notes: Short forms: 知道了 (Got it), 收到了 (Received), 好 (OK), 沒問題 (No problem).
english_contrast: English: 'Got it, thanks!' Chinese message: 知道了，謝謝！(Know LE, thanks!). Much shorter than spoken form.
korean_contrast: Korean messages also use short forms: '알았어, 고마워!' Chinese message style is similarly abbreviated.

pattern: Short confirmations: 好 / 可以 / 沒問題 / 了解
explanation_en: In messages and quick conversations, short confirmations are the norm. 好 (hǎo, 'OK'), 可以 (kěyǐ, 'can do'), 沒問題 (méi wèntí, 'no problem'), and 了解 (liǎojiě, 'understood') are the most common. These are essential for efficient communication.
title: Short Confirmations
meaning: OK; can do; no problem; understood
when_to_use: When quickly confirming plans or information
In text messages and chat apps
In casual conversation to acknowledge
when_not_to_use: Do not use 了解 in very casual settings with close friends — it can sound cold
好 is the most versatile and natural confirmation
pragmatic_notes: Taiwan LINE messages often consist of single-word confirmations. 好 is the most common, followed by 好的 (hǎo de, slightly more formal) and 沒問題.
word_order_notes: Single-word confirmations are complete responses in message contexts.

korean_contrast: Korean messages: '알겠어!' / '좋아!' Chinese 好 corresponds to Korean '좋아/알겠어'.

pattern: 不好意思 + message / 抱歉 + message
explanation_en: Use 不好意思 (bù hǎo yìsi) or 抱歉 (bàoqiàn) at the start of a message to apologize or preface something. In messages, these are used for late replies, declining invitations, or delivering bad news gently.
title: Apologetic Messages with 不好意思/抱歉
meaning: sorry; excuse me (in messages)
when_to_use: When replying late to a message
When declining an invitation via message
When delivering disappointing news gently
when_not_to_use: 不好意思 is softer than 抱歉 in messages
抱歉 is more formal and carries more weight
pragmatic_notes: Taiwan messaging culture uses 不好意思 frequently. A late reply often starts with 不好意思，現在才回 (Sorry, just replying now). It's a social lubricant in digital communication.

pattern: Statement，不然 + alternative suggestion
explanation_en: Review and expansion of 不然 (bùrán). In decision contexts, 不然 is used to pivot to an alternative: 'How about this instead?' It's one of the most useful words for collaborative decision-making in Taiwan.
title: How About Instead with 不然
meaning: otherwise; how about instead
when_to_use: When brainstorming alternatives
When the first plan doesn't work
When making suggestions in a group
when_not_to_use: Do not use 不然 to completely dismiss someone's idea — it builds on the conversation
不然 + suggestion is collaborative, not confrontational
pragmatic_notes: Taiwan group decision-making often uses 不然: 不然我們... (How about we...). It's a soft, collaborative way to suggest alternatives without rejecting anyone's idea outright.
word_order_notes: Statement，不然 + Alternative.

pattern: 要不 + suggestion？/ 要不要 + verb？
explanation_en: Use 要不 (yàobù) as a shortened form of 要不要, meaning 'how about...?' or 'shall we...?'. 要不 is more casual and common in Taiwan spoken Chinese. It's the go-to word for making spontaneous suggestions.

meaning: how about...?; shall we...? (casual suggestion)
when_to_use: When making a casual suggestion
When proposing an alternative on the spot
In friendly, informal decision-making
when_not_to_use: Do not use 要不 in formal situations — use 要不要 or 要不要考慮
要不 is very casual — perfect for friends and family
pragmatic_notes: Taiwan speakers use 要不 constantly: 要不我們去逛逛？(How about we go browse?), 要不換個時間？(How about we change the time?). It's the sound of spontaneous planning.
word_order_notes: 要不 + Suggestion？

korean_contrast: Korean: '우리 밥 먹으러 갈까?' Chinese 要不 corresponds to Korean '~을까?'.

pattern: 就這樣 + 吧 / 那就這樣 + 了
explanation_en: Use 就這樣吧 (jiù zhèyàng ba) to conclude a discussion or decision, meaning 'let's go with that' or 'that's settled then'. 就這樣 means 'just like this' and 吧 softens it into a suggestion. This is how decisions get finalized in Taiwan.
title: Let's Go With That with 就這樣吧
meaning: let's go with that; that's settled; so be it
when_to_use: When finalizing a decision after discussion
When wrapping up a planning conversation
When accepting a situation and moving on
when_not_to_use: Do not use 就這樣吧 to dismiss someone — it should follow actual discussion
The 吧 is important — without it, 就這樣 sounds abrupt
pragmatic_notes: Taiwan speakers use 就這樣吧 to wrap up discussions and move forward. It signals 'we've talked enough, let's decide.' 那就這樣了 is the version for past decisions.
word_order_notes: 那就這樣 + 吧/了.
english_contrast: English: 'Let's go with that, then.' Chinese: 那就這樣吧。(Then just this-way BA).
korean_contrast: Korean: '그럼 그렇게 하죠.' Chinese 就這樣吧 corresponds to Korean '그럼 그렇게 하죠/합시다'.

pattern: Subject + 來台灣 + 多久了？/ Subject + 來 + place + duration + 了
explanation_en: Use 來 + place + 多久了 (lái...duō jiǔ le) to ask 'How long have you been in [place]?' 來 means 'come' and 多久了 asks about duration. This is one of the most common questions foreigners get in Taiwan.

meaning: How long have you been in...?; How long has it been since you came?

When talking about duration of stay

pattern: 為了 + goal，+ action / Action + 是為了 + goal
explanation_en: Use 為了 (wèile) to express 'for the sake of' or 'in order to'. It introduces the purpose or goal of an action. This is the standard way to explain motivations and life goals in Taiwan Mandarin.

when_to_use: When explaining why you do something

pattern: Subject + 學 + language / Subject + 在學 + language / Subject + 想 + 學好 + language
explanation_en: Discussing Chinese learning goals is a universal topic for foreigners in Taiwan. 學中文 (xué Zhōngwén) means 'learn Chinese', 在學 means 'currently learning', and 想學好 means 'want to learn well'. These patterns are essential for daily conversation.
title: Learning Chinese Goals
meaning: learning Chinese; studying Chinese; wanting to master Chinese
when_to_use: When Taiwanese people ask about your Chinese learning
When discussing your language goals
When explaining your motivation for being in Taiwan
when_not_to_use: Do not say 我在學習中文 — 在學中文 is more natural
學中文 is the standard phrase, 學習中文 is more formal
pragmatic_notes: Every foreigner in Taiwan gets asked about their Chinese learning journey. 你在學中文嗎？(Are you learning Chinese?) and 你的中文很好！(Your Chinese is great!) are daily occurrences.
word_order_notes: Subject + 在學 + Language / Subject + 想 + 學好 + Language.

說 '我想學好中文'
production_drills: 跟台灣人說你在學中文
common_error_patterns: Using zai with stative verbs like need, like, or know.
Forgetting that zai marks an action happening right now.
Putting zai after the verb.

repair_feedback.word_order: Put zai or zhengzai before the action verb.
repair_feedback.meaning: Use progressive markers only for ongoing actions, not states or preferences.
repair_feedback.practice: Sort five verbs into action-now or state, then make progressive sentences only for actions.

drill_examples.translation_en: I'm learning Chinese.
I want to learn Chinese well.

pattern: 對了，+ new topic / 說到了 + topic，+ comment
explanation_en: Use 對了 (duì le) to smoothly shift to a new topic, meaning 'by the way' or 'oh, I just remembered'. This is the most natural conversation transition in Taiwan Mandarin. It signals that something just occurred to you.
title: By the Way with 對了
meaning: by the way; oh, I just remembered; speaking of which
when_to_use: When you want to introduce a new topic naturally
When something reminds you of something else
When making a smooth conversation transition
when_not_to_use: Do not overuse 對了 — it should introduce genuinely new or remembered topics
對了 is casual — in formal settings, use 另外 (lìngwài, 'additionally')
pragmatic_notes: Taiwan speakers use 對了 constantly as a conversation pivot. It's the verbal equivalent of 'oh, by the way...'. Using it naturally makes conversations flow much better.
word_order_notes: 對了，+ New Topic.
english_contrast: English: 'By the way, have you eaten yet?' Chinese: 對了，你吃飯了嗎？(Right LE, you eat rice LE MA?).
korean_contrast: Korean: '아 맞다, 밥 먹었어요?' Chinese 對了 corresponds to Korean '아 맞다/그런데'.

correct_examples.pinyin: Duì le, nǐ míngtiān yǒu kòng ma?
correct_examples.translation_en: By the way, are you free tomorrow?

correct_examples.text: 對了，我差點忘了跟你說。
correct_examples.pinyin: Duì le, wǒ chàdiǎn wàng le gēn nǐ shuō.
correct_examples.translation_en: Oh, I almost forgot to tell you.

correct_examples.text: 對了，你知道那家新開的餐廳嗎？
correct_examples.pinyin: Duì le, nǐ zhīdào nà jiā xīn kāi de cāntīng ma?
correct_examples.translation_en: By the way, do you know about that newly opened restaurant?

negative_examples.error: This word order is not correct Mandarin.

transformation_drills: 用 '對了' 自然轉話題
用 '說到了' 連接相關話題
production_drills: 聊天時想到一件事用 '對了' 開頭
common_error_patterns: Using le with every past-time sentence even when the sentence only describes a state.
Putting le immediately after the subject instead of after the completed verb phrase.
Forgetting a clear time word when the learner wants to emphasize completed action.

repair_feedback.word_order: Put the time expression before the verb phrase, then put le after the completed action/object.
repair_feedback.meaning: Use le for a bounded completed event, not just because English uses past tense.
repair_feedback.practice: Repair one past-tense English sentence by deciding first: completed event or background state?

drill_examples.translation_en: By the way, are you free tomorrow?

pattern: Statement about self，你呢？
explanation_en: Use 你呢 (nǐ ne) to bounce a question back to the other person, meaning 'and you?' or 'what about you?'. This is the single most useful two-character phrase for keeping conversations going in Chinese.
title: And You with 你呢
meaning: And you?; What about you?; How about you?
when_to_use: When returning a question to keep the conversation balanced
When you've answered and want to hear the other person's response
In almost every getting-to-know-you conversation
when_not_to_use: Do not use 你呢 after every single statement — alternate with other follow-up questions
你呢 is casual and friendly — use 您呢 (nín ne) for formal situations
pragmatic_notes: Taiwan conversations are a constant ping-pong of 你呢. It's how people show mutual interest. Mastering 你呢 is essential for natural back-and-forth conversation.
word_order_notes: Statement about self，你呢？
english_contrast: English: 'I'm from Korea. And you?' Chinese: 我是韓國人，你呢？(I am Korean person, you NE?).
korean_contrast: Korean: '저는 한국 사람이에요. ____ 씨는요?' Chinese 你呢 corresponds to Korean '~씨는요?'.

correct_examples.pinyin: Wǒ xǐhuān kàn diànyǐng, nǐ ne?
correct_examples.translation_en: I like watching movies. And you?

correct_examples.pinyin: Wǒ chī bǎo le, nǐ ne?
correct_examples.translation_en: I'm full. And you?

negative_examples.error: This word order is not correct Mandarin.

transformation_drills: 回答後用 '你呢？' 反問
用 '那你呢？' 替代
common_error_patterns: Choosing lai/qu from English perspective instead of speaker location.
Forgetting the direction complement after the main verb.
Using bring/take English one-to-one without checking movement direction.

repair_feedback.word_order: Verb + direction complement shows movement toward or away from the speaker/reference point.
repair_feedback.meaning: First choose the reference point; then choose lai or qu.
repair_feedback.practice: Describe moving one object toward you and one object away from you.

drill_examples.pinyin: Wǒ xǐhuān kàn diànyǐng, nǐ ne?
drill_examples.translation_en: I like watching movies. And you?

drill_examples.pinyin: Wǒ chī bǎo le, nǐ ne?
drill_examples.translation_en: I'm full. And you?

pattern: 順便問一下 + question / 順便 + verb
explanation_en: Use 順便 (shùnbiàn) to mean 'while we're at it' or 'incidentally'. 順便問一下 (shùnbiàn wèn yīxià) means 'while I'm asking' or 'just a quick question while we're on the topic'. This is a natural way to add a related question.
title: While We're At It with 順便
meaning: while we're at it; incidentally; conveniently
when_to_use: When adding a related question to the current topic
When doing something convenient while already there
When transitioning to a related subtopic
when_not_to_use: Do not use 順便 for completely unrelated topic changes
順便 implies the new topic is connected to what you were just discussing
pragmatic_notes: Taiwan speakers use 順便 frequently in daily life: 順便幫我買 (While you're there, buy it for me), 順便問一下 (Just a quick question while we're on the topic). It's efficient and natural.
word_order_notes: 順便 + Verb / 順便問一下 + Question.
english_contrast: English: 'While I'm here, let me ask...' Chinese: 順便問一下...(Conveniently ask YIXIA...).
korean_contrast: Korean: '가는 김에 물어볼게요.' Chinese 順便 corresponds to Korean '~는 김에'.

correct_examples.text: 順便問一下，你知道捷運站怎麼走嗎？
correct_examples.pinyin: Shùnbiàn wèn yīxià, nǐ zhīdào jiéyùn zhàn zěnme zǒu ma?
correct_examples.translation_en: While I'm asking, do you know how to get to the MRT station?

correct_examples.text: 你去超市的話，順便幫我買一瓶牛奶。
correct_examples.pinyin: Nǐ qù chāoshì dehuà, shùnbiàn bāng wǒ mǎi yī píng niúnǎi.
correct_examples.translation_en: If you're going to the supermarket, pick up a bottle of milk for me while you're there.

negative_examples.error: This word order is not correct Mandarin.

transformation_drills: 用 '順便問一下' 加問題
用 '順便' 說順路做的事
production_drills: 聊到相關話題時順便問

explanation_en: Consolidation and review of grammar patterns from days 71-85.

meaning: Review of key patterns from days 71-85.
when_to_use: When reviewing previously learned patterns.
when_not_to_use: Not a new pattern — focus on integration.
pragmatic_notes: Use this session to practice combining multiple patterns naturally in conversation.
word_order_notes: Pay attention to how different patterns interact in longer sentences.

korean_contrast: Practice producing Chinese without translating from Korean.

common_error_patterns: Keeping English question word order.
Putting ma in a sentence that already has a question word.
Using shenme when zenme or na li is needed.

repair_feedback.word_order: Keep Chinese statement word order; put the question word in the answer slot.
repair_feedback.meaning: Use ma for yes/no questions, not with shenme/na li/zenme questions.
repair_feedback.practice: Turn three statements into questions without moving the subject and verb.
